# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These questions are modeled on actual KTU 2024 ESE Part A paper patterns. Answer each in **3-4 sentences** with formulas.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What are the essential components of a public key cryptosystem? List the components of the RSA public and private keys.**

**Model Answer:**
A public-key cryptosystem uses two mathematically related but distinct keys — a public key for encryption/verification and a private key for decryption/signing. In RSA, the **public key** is the pair $(e, n)$ where $e$ is the public exponent and $n$ is the modulus, while the **private key** is the pair $(d, n)$ where $d$ is the private exponent. Both keys are derived from two secret prime factors $p$ and $q$ such that $n = p \cdot q$ and $\varphi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$. The relationship $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\varphi(n)}$ ensures decryption recovers the original message.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Why is Euler's totient function $\varphi(n)$ kept secret in RSA? What happens if it is disclosed?**

**Model Answer:**
$\varphi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ is kept secret because knowledge of $\varphi(n)$ allows any attacker to compute the private key $d = e^{-1} \mod \varphi(n)$ in polynomial time, completely breaking the cryptosystem. Furthermore, knowing $\varphi(n)$ enables efficient factorization of $n$ into $p$ and $q$ by solving the quadratic $x^2 - (n - \varphi(n) + 1)x + n = 0$. Thus, disclosure of $\varphi(n)$ is **equivalent in power** to disclosure of the private key — both must remain confidential.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part B question has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. The KTU paper presents an **OR** choice between Question A and Question B — you must answer **either** A **or** B in full.

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU Model Paper Style]

**[A] (a)** With reference to the RSA algorithm implemented in your lab, **explain the key generation process** with a suitable example using $p = 17$, $q = 11$, and $e = 7$. Compute the public key $(e, n)$ and private key $(d, n)$. **(7 marks)**

**[A] (b)** Describe the **Java program structure** used to implement RSA in your lab. List the important `java.math.BigInteger` methods used (e.g., `probablePrime`, `modPow`, `modInverse`) and explain their role in key generation, encryption, and decryption. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for [A] (a):

**Step 1:** Compute $n = p \cdot q = 17 \times 11 = 187$.

**Step 2:** Compute $\varphi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 16 \times 10 = 160$.

**Step 3:** Validate $e = 7$: $\gcd(7, 160) = 1$ ✓ (since 7 is prime and does not divide 160).

**Step 4:** Compute $d$ such that $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{160}$.

Applying the extended Euclidean algorithm:

$$160 = 7 \times 22 + 6$$
$$7 = 6 \times 1 + 1$$

Back-substitute: $1 = 7 - 6 \times 1 = 7 - (160 - 7 \times 22) = 7 \times 23 - 160 \times 1$

Therefore $d \equiv 23 \pmod{160}$. So $d = 23$.

**Step 5:** Keys are:
- **Public Key** $(e, n) = (7, 187)$
- **Private Key** $(d, n) = (23, 187)$

**Verification:** $7 \times 23 = 161 = 160 \times 1 + 1$ ✓

#### Model Solution for [A] (b):

The Java implementation uses a single class `RSA` encapsulating the BigInteger-based keypair. Key methods used:

| BigInteger Method | Mathematical Operation | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| `probablePrime(int, Random)` | Generates a probable prime of given bit length | Prime generation |
| `multiply(BigInteger)` | Product of two BigIntegers | $n = p \cdot q$ |
| `subtract(BigInteger)` | Difference of two BigIntegers | $p-1$, $q-1$ |
| `gcd(BigInteger)` | Greatest common divisor | Validating $\gcd(e, \varphi) = 1$ |
| `modInverse(BigInteger)` | Modular inverse | Computing $d = e^{-1} \mod \varphi$ |
| `modPow(BigInteger, BigInteger)` | Modular exponentiation | Both $M^e \mod n$ and $C^d \mod n$ |

The program flow is: **constructor generates keys** → **user inputs plaintext $M$** → **`encrypt()` computes $C = M^e \mod n$** → **transmit $C$** → **`decrypt()` computes $M = C^d \mod n$**. The use of `BigInteger` is mandatory because typical RSA-1024/2048 moduli far exceed the 64-bit `long` range.

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**[B] (a)** Perform **RSA encryption and decryption** for the following parameters: $p = 5$, $q = 11$, $e = 3$, plaintext $M = 9$. Show every step including key generation, encryption, decryption, and verification. **(7 marks)**

**[B] (b)** What are the **common attacks on RSA**? Explain any three — Brute Force, Mathematical Attacks, and Timing Attacks — with brief mitigations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for [B] (a):

**Step 1:** $n = 5 \times 11 = 55$.

**Step 2:** $\varphi(n) = 4 \times 10 = 40$.

**Step 3:** Validate $e = 3$: $\gcd(3, 40) = 1$ ✓.

**Step 4:** Compute $d$: $3d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$.

$$40 = 3 \times 13 + 1 \implies 1 = 40 - 3 \times 13$$

So $d \equiv -13 \equiv 27 \pmod{40}$. Thus $d = 27$.

**Verification:** $3 \times 27 = 81 = 40 \times 2 + 1$ ✓.

**Step 5:** Encrypt $M = 9$:
$$C = 9^3 \mod 55 = 729 \mod 55$$

$729 / 55 = 13.254...$, so $55 \times 13 = 715$, hence $C = 729 - 715 = 14$.

**Step 6:** Decrypt $C = 14$:
$$M = 14^{27} \mod 55$$

Using repeated squaring:
$$14^1 \equiv 14, \quad 14^2 \equiv 196 \equiv 31, \quad 14^4 \equiv 31^2 = 961 \equiv 26, \quad 14^8 \equiv 26^2 = 676 \equiv 16, \quad 14^{16} \equiv 16^2 = 256 \equiv 36$$

$27 = 16 + 8 + 2 + 1$, so $14^{27} = 14^{16} \times 14^8 \times 14^2 \times 14^1$:
$$14^{27} \equiv 36 \times 16 \times 31 \times 14 \pmod{55}$$
$$= 36 \times 16 \pmod{55} = 576 \mod 55 = 26$$
$$26 \times 31 \pmod{55} = 806 \mod 55 = 36$$
$$36 \times 14 \pmod{55} = 504 \mod 55 = 9$$

$M = 9$ ✓ (original plaintext recovered).

#### Model Solution for [B] (b):

**Common Attacks on RSA:**

1. **Brute Force Attack** — Try all possible values of $d$. **Mitigation:** Use sufficiently large keys (≥ 2048 bits) so brute force is computationally infeasible.

2. **Mathematical Attack** — Factor $n$ into $p \cdot q$ using algorithms like GNFS, then compute $d$ directly. **Mitigation:** Use large prime factors, avoid primes close together, use safe primes $p = 2q + 1$.

3. **Timing Attack** — Measure the exact time taken for modular exponentiation to deduce bit-by-bit values of $d$. **Mitigation:** Use **constant-time implementations** that perform operations in fixed time regardless of secret data, or add random cryptographic blinding: $M' = M \cdot r^e \mod n$ before decryption.

4. **Chosen Ciphertext Attack** — Exploit the homomorphic property $E(M_1) \cdot E(M_2) \equiv E(M_1 \cdot M_2) \pmod{n}$. **Mitigation:** Use proper padding schemes like **OAEP** (Optimal Asymmetric Encryption Padding).

5. **Low Exponent Attack** — If $e = 3$ and $M^3 < n$, the ciphertext is just $M^3$ in integers. **Mitigation:** Use OAEP padding or larger $e$ (typically $e = 65537$).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `(p-1)(q-1)` formula:** Many students incorrectly write $\varphi(n) = p \cdot q$, costing 3 marks.
> 2. **Sign errors in extended Euclidean:** Double-check the back-substitution; a sign error propagates and makes $d$ wrong.
> 3. **Java-specific:** Students often write `Math.pow()` instead of `modPow()`. The former is floating-point, useless for cryptography. Use `BigInteger.modPow()` always.
> 4. **Encrypting $M \geq n$:** If plaintext is larger than modulus, modular reduction silently corrupts the message. Always ensure $M < n$.
> 5. **Skipping the `gcd` check:** If $\gcd(e, \varphi) \ne 1$, then $d$ does not exist and the program throws `ArithmeticException`. Always validate.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — RSA Algorithm (KTU 2024 Lab Experiment 2):**

- ✅ RSA is an **asymmetric** (public-key) cryptosystem by **Rivest, Shamir, Adleman (1977)**.
- ✅ **Public Key** = $(e, n)$; **Private Key** = $(d, n)$; **Modulus** $n = p \cdot q$.
- ✅ **Euler's Totient** $\varphi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ — must be kept **secret**.
- ✅ **Public exponent** $e$ is chosen with $1 < e < \varphi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \varphi) = 1$; default is $e = 65537$.
- ✅ **Private exponent** $d = e^{-1} \mod \varphi(n)$ via the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**.
- ✅ **Encryption:** $C = M^e \mod n$. **Decryption:** $M = C^d \mod n$.
- ✅ **Correctness** depends on Euler's theorem: $a^{\varphi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod n$.
- ✅ **Signing:** $S = M^d \mod n$. **Verification:** recover via $S^e \mod n$.
- ✅ In Java, always use **`java.math.BigInteger`** — never `int` or `long` for cryptographic values.
- ✅ Key method: **`modPow(exponent, modulus)`** for modular exponentiation.
- ✅ Key method: **`modInverse(m)`** for computing $d$.
- ✅ Key method: **`probablePrime(bits, random)`** for prime generation.
- ✅ **Minimum recommended key size:** 2048 bits (1024 bits is the KTU lab-friendly minimum for speed).
- ✅ **Real-world deployment:** TLS/SSL, PGP, JWT (RS256), SSH, X.509 certificates.
- ✅ **Vulnerabilities to mention in viva:** small key size, $p$ and $q$ too close, low $e$ without padding, timing attacks.
- ✅ **Standard padding:** OAEP (PKCS#1 v2.2); never use textbook RSA on raw data in production.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implement the Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange mechanism using HTML and JavaScript. Consider the end user as one of the parties (Alice) and the JavaScript application as other party (Bob).
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCGL508 - FOUNDATIONS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Implement the Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange mechanism using HTML and JavaScript. Consider the end user as one of the parties (Alice) and the JavaScript application as other party (Bob). ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange (DHKE)** is a public-key cryptographic protocol proposed by **Whitfield Diffie** and **Martin Hellman** in **1976**. It enables two parties communicating over an *insecure public channel* to jointly establish a **shared symmetric secret key** without any prior shared secret material. The protocol's security relies on the computational intractability of the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** in a finite cyclic group. Given a large prime $p$, a primitive root $g$, and the value $Y = g^x \bmod p$, it is computationally infeasible to recover $x$ for sufficiently large $p$ (typically **2048-bit or larger** in modern deployments like TLS 1.3). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Shared Paint Bucket" Imagine **Alice** and **Bob** each have a **private color** (secret) that nobody else knows. The protocol works as follows: 1. They publicly agree on a **common base color** (public prime $p$ and generator $g$). 2. **Alice** mixes her private color into the base → produces **Mixture A** (her public key $A$). She sends this openly to Bob. 3. **Bob** mixes his private color into the base → produces **Mixture B** (his public key $B$). He sends this openly to Alice. 4. **Alice** takes Bob's Mixture B and adds her *private* color → final shared color. 5. **Bob** takes Alice's Mixture A and adds his *private* color → **identical** final shared color. A passive eavesdropper **Eve** sees only the mixtures and the base color, but **cannot reverse-engineer** the private colors from the mixtures — this is the essence of the **Discrete Logarithm trapdoor**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** DHKE is a *key establishment* protocol, NOT an encryption algorithm. The shared secret produced is then typically used as the key for a symmetric cipher (like AES) or for HMAC operations. ### 1.3 Experiment Context (KTU Lab Specification) | Role | Entity | Responsibility | |------|--------|----------------| | **Alice** | End User (Human) | Inputs prime $p$, generator $g$, secret $a$ via HTML form | | **Bob** | JavaScript Application | Auto-generates secret $b$, performs modular exponentiation | | **Channel** | Browser internal computation | Simulates the public channel between Alice and Bob | The experiment demonstrates that both parties — the human (Alice) and the script (Bob) — **arrive at the same shared secret $K$**, despite communicating only public values. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Algorithmic Breakdown (Operational Steps) The DHKE protocol proceeds through five logically distinct phases. Let us dissect the *why* and *how* behind each: **Phase 1 — Public Parameter Agreement** - Alice (user) selects a large prime $p$ and a primitive root $g$ (generator of $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$). - These values are **public** — they could be transmitted in cleartext without compromising security. - *Why?* Both parties need a common mathematical playground (cyclic group) to perform their computations. **Phase 2 — Private Key Generation** - Alice secretly picks an integer $a$ where $1 \le a \le p-2$. - JavaScript (Bob) secretly picks an integer $b$ where $1 \le b \le p-2$. - *Why random?* Predictability of secrets would allow an attacker to brute-force the key. **Phase 3 — Public Key Computation & Exchange** - Alice computes: $A = g^a \bmod p$ and sends $A$ over the (insecure) public channel. - Bob computes: $B = g^b \bmod p$ and sends $B$ over the (insecure) public channel. - *Why modular exponentiation?* It makes direct inversion (finding $a$ from $A$) computationally hard. **Phase 4 — Shared Secret Derivation** - Alice receives $B$ and computes: $K_A = B^a \bmod p$. - Bob receives $A$ and computes: $K_B = A^b \bmod p$. - *Why do they match?* Because of the commutative property of exponents: $(g^b)^a = (g^a)^b = g^{ab} \bmod p$. **Phase 5 — Verification** - The application displays both $K_A$ and $K_B$ side-by-side to confirm equality. - The shared secret is then usable for symmetric encryption (e.g., AES key derivation). ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize the following table — these are the **four pillars** of every DHKE question in KTU exams. | Step | Formula | Variable Meaning | Computation Type | |------|---------|------------------|------------------| | Public Modulus | $p$ | Large prime number (public) | Pre-agreed | | Generator | $g$ | Primitive root mod $p$ (public) | Pre-agreed | | Alice's Public Key | $A \equiv g^a \pmod{p}$ | $a$ is Alice's private secret | Modular Exponentiation | | Bob's Public Key | $B \equiv g^b \pmod{p}$ | $b$ is Bob's private secret | Modular Exponentiation | | Alice's Computed Secret | $K_A \equiv B^a \pmod{p}$ | Uses received $B$ | Modular Exponentiation | | Bob's Computed Secret | $K_B \equiv A^b \pmod{p}$ | Uses received $A$ | Modular Exponentiation | | Shared Secret Equivalence | $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$ | Final symmetric key | Result | **Security Hardness Assumptions:** - **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP):** Given $p$, $g$, and $Y = g^x \bmod p$, find $x$. Considered hard for $\vert p \vert \ge 2048$ bits. - **Computational Diffie-Hellman Problem (CDHP):** Given $g^a$ and $g^b$, compute $g^{ab}$. Also hard under DLP assumption. - **No Authentication:** DHKE is vulnerable to **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attacks** if used without authentication — this is why modern implementations pair it with digital signatures (e.g., ECDHE in TLS). ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications 1. **TLS 1.3 Handshake** — Uses **Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman Ephemeral (ECDHE)** for perfect forward secrecy. 2. **IPsec VPN Tunnels** — DHKE negotiates session keys between VPN endpoints (IKEv2 protocol). 3. **SSH Protocol** — Initial key exchange uses DHKE variants. 4. **Signal Protocol (WhatsApp, Signal)** — Uses X3DH (Extended Triple Diffie-Hellman) for end-to-end encryption bootstrapping. 5. **PGP / GPG** — Some implementations support DHKE for key agreement. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Mathematical Proof of Shared Secret Equivalence This is the **most important derivation** for KTU exams. We must prove that $K_A = K_B$ despite being computed from different inputs. Starting from the public parameters: $p$ (prime), $g$ (generator), and the exchanged public keys $A$ and $B$. $$ K_A = B^a \bmod p $$ Substitute $B = g^b \bmod p$: $$ K_A = (g^b)^a \bmod p $$ Apply the power rule $(x^y)^z = x^{yz}$: $$ K_A = g^{b \cdot a} \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p $$ Now starting from Bob's side: $$ K_B = A^b \bmod p $$ Substitute $A = g^a \bmod p$: $$ K_B = (g^a)^b \bmod p $$ Apply the power rule: $$ K_B = g^{a \cdot b} \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p $$ Since multiplication of integers is commutative ($ab = ba$): $$ K_A = g^{ab} \bmod p = K_B \quad \blacksquare $$ > [!NOTE] > The entire security of DHKE rests on this algebraic commutativity. The eavesdropper sees $g^a$ and $g^b$, but cannot efficiently compute $g^{ab}$ without knowing either $a$ or $b$. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Textbook Problem) Let us work through the canonical example: $p = 23$, $g = 5$, $a = 6$, $b = 15$. **Step 1 — Compute Alice's Public Key $A$:** $$ A = g^a \bmod p = 5^6 \bmod 23 $$ Compute $5^6$: $$ 5^2 = 25, \quad 5^3 = 125, \quad 5^4 = 625, \quad 5^5 = 3125, \quad 5^6 = 15625 $$ Apply modulo 23: $$ 15625 \div 23 = 679.347... \quad \Rightarrow \quad 679 \times 23 = 15617 $$ $$ A = 15625 - 15617 = 8 $$ **Step 2 — Compute Bob's Public Key $B$:** $$ B = g^b \bmod p = 5^{15} \bmod 23 $$ Using repeated squaring for efficiency (more practical for large exponents): $$ 5^1 \bmod 23 = 5 $$ $$ 5^2 \bmod 23 = 25 \bmod 23 = 2 $$ $$ 5^4 \bmod 23 = (5^2)^2 \bmod 23 = 2^2 \bmod 23 = 4 $$ $$ 5^8 \bmod 23 = (5^4)^2 \bmod 23 = 4^2 \bmod 23 = 16 $$ Now $15 = 8 + 4 + 2 + 1$, so: $$ 5^{15} = 5^8 \times 5^4 \times 5^2 \times 5^1 = 16 \times 4 \times 2 \times 5 = 640 $$ $$ 640 \bmod 23: \quad 23 \times 27 = 621, \quad 640 - 621 = 19 $$ $$ B = 19 $$ **Step 3 — Alice Computes Shared Secret $K_A$:** Alice receives $B = 19$ and applies her secret $a = 6$: $$ K_A = B^a \bmod p = 19^6 \bmod 23 $$ Compute $19^6 \bmod 23$ via repeated squaring: $$ 19^1 \bmod 23 = 19 $$ $$ 19^2 \bmod 23 = 361 \bmod 23 = 16 $$ $$ 19^4 \bmod 23 = 16^2 \bmod 23 = 256 \bmod 23 = 3 $$ Now $6 = 4 + 2$: $$ 19^6 = 19^4 \times 19^2 = 3 \times 16 = 48 $$ $$ 48 \bmod 23 = 48 - 23 = 25, \quad 25 - 23 = 2 $$ $$ K_A = 2 $$ **Step 4 — Bob Computes Shared Secret $K_B$:** Bob receives $A = 8$ and applies his secret $b = 15$: $$ K_B = A^b \bmod p = 8^{15} \bmod 23 $$ Compute via repeated squaring: $$ 8^1 \bmod 23 = 8 $$ $$ 8^2 \bmod 23 = 64 \bmod 23 = 18 $$ $$ 8^4 \bmod 23 = 18^2 \bmod 23 = 324 \bmod 23 = 324 - 322 = 2 $$ $$ 8^8 \bmod 23 = 2^2 \bmod 23 = 4 $$ Now $15 = 8 + 4 + 2 + 1$: $$ 8^{15} = 8^8 \times 8^4 \times 8^2 \times 8^1 = 4 \times 2 \times 18 \times 8 = 1152 $$ $$ 1152 \bmod 23: \quad 23 \times 50 = 1150, \quad 1152 - 1150 = 2 $$ $$ K_B = 2 $$ **Step 5 — Verification:** $$ K_A = K_B = 2 \quad \checkmark $$ Both Alice and Bob — using *different inputs* — converged to the **same shared secret key** $K = 2$. ### 3.3 Complete HTML + JavaScript Implementation (Lab Code) Below is the **fully working** HTML/JavaScript code for the experiment. Save as `dhke.html` and open in any modern browser. **HTML Structure (dhke.html):** ```html Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange - Lab Experiment 2

🔐 Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange

Alice (You) ↔ Bob (JavaScript)

👩 Alice (End User)

👨 Bob (JavaScript Application)

Click "Start Key Exchange" to begin...

🔑 Shared Secret Verification

Awaiting exchange...
``` ### 3.4 Code Walkthrough (Analytical Matrix) | Code Block | Purpose | KTU Concept Tested | |------------|---------|---------------------| | `modPow(base, exp, mod)` | Implements **fast modular exponentiation** using binary method (square-and-multiply) | Algorithm design, $O(\log n)$ complexity | | `isPrime(n)` | Validates that $p$ is indeed a prime number before proceeding | Input validation, number theory | | `parseInt(...)` + bounds checks | Ensures $1 \le a < p$, $2 \le g < p$ | Range constraints, security correctness | | `Math.floor(Math.random() * (p-2)) + 1` | Generates Bob's secret $b$ in valid range | Cryptographic randomness | | `modPow(g, a, p)` | Computes Alice's public key $A = g^a \bmod p$ | Core DHKE public key step | | `modPow(B, a, p)` & `modPow(A, b, p)` | Computes both shared secrets | Convergence proof demonstration | | `match` boolean check | Validates algebraic equality | Verification of commutativity property | ### 3.5 Expected Output (Using Default Values $p=23$, $g=5$, $a=6$) **Alice (User Input):** - $p = 23$, $g = 5$, $a = 6$ - Alice's Public Key: $A = 5^6 \bmod 23 = 8$ **Bob (JavaScript Auto-Generated, e.g., $b = 15$):** - Bob's Secret: $b = 15$ - Bob's Public Key: $B = 5^{15} \bmod 23 = 19$ **Final Shared Secret:** - Alice computes: $K_A = 19^6 \bmod 23 = 2$ - Bob computes: $K_B = 8^{15} \bmod 23 = 2$ - ✅ **Keys Match! $K = 2$** --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Diffie-Hellman Protocol Flow (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Alice End User HTML Form] nodeB[JavaScript Application Bob] nodeC[Public Channel Browser] nodeD[Shared Secret Convergence] nodeA -- Step 1 Inputs p g a --> nodeC nodeC -- Step 2 Receives params --> nodeB nodeB -- Step 3 Generates random b --> nodeB nodeB -- Step 4 Computes A equals g to the power a mod p --> nodeB nodeA -- Step 5 Computes A equals g to the power a mod p --> nodeA nodeA -- Step 6 Sends A to Bob --> nodeB nodeB -- Step 7 Sends B to Alice --> nodeA nodeA -- Step 8 Alice computes K equals B to the power a mod p --> nodeA nodeB -- Step 9 Bob computes K equals A to the power b mod p --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeD nodeB --> nodeD nodeD -- Step 10 Both arrive at same K --> nodeD ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the HTML/JS Application ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph inputLayer [USER INPUT LAYER] input1[Prime p input field] input2[Generator g input field] input3[Alice Secret a input field] btn[Start Exchange Button] end subgraph validationLayer [VALIDATION LAYER] v1[Prime Check] v2[Range Check on g and a] end subgraph bobLayer [BOB JAVASCRIPT LAYER] gen[Random b Generator] calc1[Compute B equals g to the power b mod p] calc2[Compute K_B equals A to the power b mod p] end subgraph aliceLayer [ALICE COMPUTATION LAYER] calc3[Compute A equals g to the power a mod p] calc4[Compute K_A equals B to the power a mod p] end subgraph outputLayer [OUTPUT RENDERING LAYER] out1[Bob Panel Display] out2[Shared Secret Result] out3[Match Verification Badge] end input1 --> v1 input2 --> v2 input3 --> v2 v1 --> calc3 v2 --> calc3 btn --> calc3 btn --> gen gen --> calc1 calc1 --> out1 calc3 --> out1 out1 --> calc4 out1 --> calc2 calc2 --> out2 calc4 --> out2 out2 --> out3 ``` ### 4.3 Conceptual Data Flow (Modular Exponentiation Path) ```mermaid flowchart TD start[User Clicks Start Exchange] --> read[Read p g a from DOM] read --> valid{Valid Input} valid -- No --> err[Show Error Message] valid -- Yes --> randb[Generate Bob secret b in range 1 to p minus 2] randb --> compA[Compute A equals modPow g a p] compA --> compB[Compute B equals modPow g b p] compB --> exchA[Send A to Bob over simulated channel] exchA --> exchB[Send B to Alice over simulated channel] exchB --> kA[Alice computes K_A equals modPow B a p] exchB --> kB[Bob computes K_B equals modPow A b p] kA --> compare{K_A equals K_B} kB --> compare compare -- Yes --> success[Display SUCCESS shared secret K] compare -- No --> fail[Display FAILED mismatch alert] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. Define the Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange protocol. List the public and private parameters involved. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** **Model Answer:** Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange (DHKE) is a cryptographic protocol that allows two parties to establish a shared symmetric secret key over an insecure public communication channel without having met beforehand or exchanged any prior secret material. It was proposed by Diffie and Hellman in 1976 and is based on the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)**. **Public parameters:** Prime $p$, primitive root (generator) $g$. **Private (secret) parameters:** Alice's secret $a$, Bob's secret $b$. **Public key parameters (exchanged):** $A = g^a \bmod p$, $B = g^b \bmod p$. The protocol achieves its goal because the computed shared secret $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$ is identical when derived by either party, yet infeasible for an eavesdropper to recover. --- **Q2. What is the Discrete Logarithm Problem, and why is it central to the security of Diffie-Hellman? `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** **Model Answer:** The **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** is defined as follows: given a prime $p$, a generator $g$ of $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$, and an element $Y \in \mathbb{Z}_p^*$ such that $Y = g^x \bmod p$, find the integer $x$. DLP is the **security foundation** of DHKE because: 1. Computing $Y = g^x \bmod p$ is **easy** (efficient modular exponentiation in $O(\log p \cdot (\log x)^2)$ time). 2. Recovering $x$ from $Y$ (the inverse) is **computationally infeasible** for large primes (sub-exponential algorithms like Index Calculus require $O(e^{1.92 \log^{1/3} p \cdot \log^{2/3} \log p})$ bit operations). 3. An eavesdropper observing $g^a$ and $g^b$ cannot compute $g^{ab}$ without solving the **Computational Diffie-Hellman Problem (CDHP)**, which reduces to DLP. For $p$ of size 2048 bits or more, DLP is considered intractable, making DHKE secure. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q1 (a). Explain the step-by-step procedure of the Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange algorithm. Clearly identify the public parameters, private parameters, and the final shared secret derivation. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Public Parameter Generation:** Two parties (Alice and Bob) publicly agree on a large prime number $p$ and a primitive root $g$ modulo $p$ (where $2 \le g \le p-2$). These are the *public parameters*. **Step 2 — Private Key Selection:** - Alice selects a secret integer $a$ such that $1 \le a \le p-2$. - Bob selects a secret integer $b$ such that $1 \le b \le p-2$. - These are *private* and never transmitted. **Step 3 — Public Key Computation:** - Alice computes: $A = g^a \bmod p$. - Bob computes: $B = g^b \bmod p$. **Step 4 — Public Key Exchange:** - Alice sends $A$ to Bob over the public channel. - Bob sends $B$ to Alice over the public channel. **Step 5 — Shared Secret Computation:** - Alice receives $B$ and computes: $K_A = B^a \bmod p$. - Bob receives $A$ and computes: $K_B = A^b \bmod p$. **Step 6 — Convergence:** Mathematically, $K_A = (g^b)^a \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p = (g^a)^b \bmod p = K_B$. Both parties now hold the **identical shared symmetric key** $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$ suitable for use with symmetric ciphers. --- **Q1 (b). In a Diffie-Hellman exchange, $p = 353$, $g = 3$, $a = 97$, and $b = 233$. Compute the public keys $A$ and $B$, and verify that both parties arrive at the same shared secret $K$. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Compute Alice's Public Key $A$:** $$ A = g^a \bmod p = 3^{97} \bmod 353 $$ Using repeated squaring: $$ 3^1 \bmod 353 = 3 $$ $$ 3^2 \bmod 353 = 9 $$ $$ 3^4 \bmod 353 = 81 $$ $$ 3^8 \bmod 353 = 81^2 \bmod 353 = 6561 \bmod 353 $$ $353 \times 18 = 6354$, so $6561 - 6354 = 207$. $$ 3^8 \bmod 353 = 207 $$ $$ 3^{16} \bmod 353 = 207^2 \bmod 353 = 42849 \bmod 353 $$ $353 \times 121 = 42713$, so $42849 - 42713 = 136$. $$ 3^{16} \bmod 353 = 136 $$ $$ 3^{32} \bmod 353 = 136^2 \bmod 353 = 18496 \bmod 353 $$ $353 \times 52 = 18356$, so $18496 - 18356 = 140$. $$ 3^{32} \bmod 353 = 140 $$ $$ 3^{64} \bmod 353 = 140^2 \bmod 353 = 19600 \bmod 353 $$ $353 \times 55 = 19415$, so $19600 - 19415 = 185$. $$ 3^{64} \bmod 353 = 185 $$ Now $97 = 64 + 32 + 1$: $$ 3^{97} = 3^{64} \times 3^{32} \times 3^1 = 185 \times 140 \times 3 = 77700 $$ $77700 \bmod 353$: $353 \times 220 = 77660$, so $77700 - 77660 = 40$. $$ A = 40 $$ **Compute Bob's Public Key $B$:** $$ B = g^b \bmod p = 3^{233} \bmod 353 $$ Continuing the binary exponentiation with $233 = 128 + 64 + 32 + 8 + 1$: $$ 3^{128} \bmod 353 = (3^{64})^2 \bmod 353 = 185^2 \bmod 353 = 34225 \bmod 353 $$ $353 \times 96 = 33888$, so $34225 - 33888 = 337$. $$ 3^{128} \bmod 353 = 337 $$ Now combine powers: $$ 3^{233} = 3^{128} \times 3^{64} \times 3^{32} \times 3^{8} \times 3^1 $$ $$ = 337 \times 185 \times 140 \times 207 \times 3 $$ Computing step by step: $$ 337 \times 185 = 62345 $$ $$ 62345 \bmod 353: \quad 353 \times 176 = 62128, \quad 62345 - 62128 = 217 $$ $$ 217 \times 140 = 30380 $$ $$ 30380 \bmod 353: \quad 353 \times 86 = 30358, \quad 30380 - 30358 = 22 $$ $$ 22 \times 207 = 4554 $$ $$ 4554 \bmod 353: \quad 353 \times 12 = 4236, \quad 4554 - 4236 = 318 $$ $$ 318 \times 3 = 954 $$ $$ 954 \bmod 353: \quad 353 \times 2 = 706, \quad 954 - 706 = 248 $$ $$ B = 248 $$ **Compute Shared Secret $K$:** Alice computes $K_A = B^a \bmod p = 248^{97} \bmod 353$. Bob computes $K_B = A^b \bmod p = 40^{233} \bmod 353$. By the algebraic property of commutativity: $$ K_A = K_B = g^{ab} \bmod p = 3^{97 \times 233} \bmod 353 = 3^{22601} \bmod 353 $$ Using programmatic evaluation (or further repeated squaring): $K = 160$. Both parties arrive at the **same shared secret** $K = 160$. $\checkmark$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q2 (a). Prove mathematically that both parties in a Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange arrive at the same shared secret despite starting with different inputs. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Given:** - Public parameters: prime $p$, generator $g$. - Alice's secret: $a$; Bob's secret: $b$. - Alice computes and sends: $A = g^a \bmod p$. - Bob computes and sends: $B = g^b \bmod p$. **To Prove:** $K_A = K_B$ where $K_A = B^a \bmod p$ and $K_B = A^b \bmod p$. **Proof:** Starting with Alice's computation: $$ K_A \equiv B^a \pmod{p} $$ Substitute $B \equiv g^b \pmod{p}$: $$ K_A \equiv (g^b)^a \pmod{p} $$ By the power rule $(x^m)^n = x^{m \cdot n}$: $$ K_A \equiv g^{b \cdot a} \pmod{p} \equiv g^{ab} \pmod{p} $$ Now starting with Bob's computation: $$ K_B \equiv A^b \pmod{p} $$ Substitute $A \equiv g^a \pmod{p}$: $$ K_B \equiv (g^a)^b \pmod{p} $$ By the power rule: $$ K_B \equiv g^{a \cdot b} \pmod{p} \equiv g^{ab} \pmod{p} $$ Since integer multiplication is commutative ($a \cdot b = b \cdot a$): $$ K_A \equiv g^{ab} \pmod{p} \equiv K_B \quad \blacksquare $$ Hence, both parties independently arrive at the **identical shared secret** $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$. **Security Implication:** An eavesdropper observing $A = g^a$ and $B = g^b$ would need to solve the Discrete Logarithm Problem to recover $a$ or $b$, and then compute $g^{ab}$. For sufficiently large $p$ (2048+ bits), this is computationally infeasible. --- **Q2 (b). Describe the architecture of a Diffie-Hellman implementation where Alice is the end user interacting with an HTML form and Bob is implemented as a JavaScript application. Include code snippets and explain each functional module. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Architecture Overview:** The application has three logical layers: 1. **Presentation Layer (HTML):** Provides the user interface for Alice. 2. **Logic Layer (JavaScript):** Acts as Bob — generates secret, computes modular exponentiations. 3. **Output Layer (DOM Manipulation):** Displays the final shared secret and verification status. **Key Functional Modules:** **Module 1 — Input Capture (HTML):** ```html ``` This module lets Alice (the human) provide her inputs. Defaults like $p = 23$, $g = 5$ are pre-filled for ease of testing. **Module 2 — Modular Exponentiation (JavaScript core):** ```javascript function modPow(base, exp, mod) { let result = 1; base = base % mod; while (exp > 0) { if (exp % 2 === 1) { result = (result * base) % mod; } exp = Math.floor(exp / 2); base = (base * base) % mod; } return result; } ``` This is the **square-and-multiply** algorithm running in $O(\log \text{exp})$ multiplications — essential for handling large primes efficiently. **Module 3 — Bob's Secret Generation:** ```javascript const b = Math.floor(Math.random() * (p - 2)) + 1; ``` Bob's private secret $b$ is randomly generated in the valid range $[1, p-2]$. **Module 4 — Exchange Computation:** ```javascript const A = modPow(g, a, p); // Alice's public key const B = modPow(g, b, p); // Bob's public key const aliceShared = modPow(B, a, p); // Alice: B^a mod p const bobShared = modPow(A, b, p); // Bob: A^b mod p ``` **Module 5 — Verification & Display:** ```javascript const match = (aliceShared === bobShared); document.getElementById('resultPanel').innerHTML = `Shared Secret K = ${aliceShared}. Match: ${match ? 'YES' : 'NO'}`; ``` **Outcome:** The browser console and on-screen panel both confirm that $K_A = K_B$, demonstrating the protocol's correctness in a controlled single-machine environment. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in DHKE Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing $a$ with $A$:** Students often interchange the *private secret* $a$ and the *public key* $A = g^a \bmod p$. Remember: lowercase = secret, uppercase = public key. > 2. **Skipping the modulo operation:** Writing $K = g^{ab}$ without $\bmod p$ loses 1–2 marks. The protocol is **insecure** without the modulo reduction. > 3. **Forgetting the "exchange" step:** DHKE is an *interactive* protocol — you must explicitly show that $A$ is sent to Bob and $B$ is sent to Alice. Merely computing $g^a$ and $g^b$ is NOT a complete key exchange. > 4. **Ignoring the DLP security argument:** KTU 2024 schemes place heavy weight on *why* the protocol is secure. Always state the **Discrete Logarithm assumption** explicitly. > 5. **Not validating $p$ as prime:** In code-based questions, failing to check that $p$ is prime is a 1-mark deduction. > 6. **MITM vulnerability not mentioned:** A full-marks answer should briefly note that **unauthenticated DHKE is vulnerable to Man-in-the-Middle attacks** — and mention that authenticated variants (e.g., Station-to-Station protocol, digital signatures) are used in practice. > 7. **Modular arithmetic calculation errors:** Repeated squaring is mandatory for large exponents; full expansion of $3^{97}$ is impractical. Examiners expect efficient computation strategies. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — DHKE Lab Experiment 2** - **DHKE is a key agreement protocol, NOT encryption.** It establishes a *shared secret* for subsequent symmetric operations. - **The five inputs/outputs:** $p$ (prime), $g$ (generator), $a$, $b$, $A$, $B$, $K$. Know each one's classification (public vs. private). - **The four core formulas:** $A = g^a \bmod p$, $B = g^b \bmod p$, $K_A = B^a \bmod p$, $K_B = A^b \bmod p$. - **The master equation:** $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$ — derivable from BOTH sides due to commutativity. - **Security basis:** Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP) on $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$. - **MITM attack vulnerability:** DHKE provides NO authentication by itself — must be paired with digital signatures. - **Practical key sizes:** $p \ge 2048$ bits; modern variants use Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman (ECDH) with 256-bit curves. - **Code essentials for lab:** - Use `modPow()` (square-and-multiply) — never naive exponentiation. - Validate that $p$ is prime, $2 \le g < p$, $1 \le a, b < p$. - Display both $K_A$ and $K_B$ to demonstrate convergence. - **Classical example to memorize:** $p=23$, $g=5$, $a=6$, $b=15 \Rightarrow A=8$, $B=19$, $K=2$. This appears in nearly every textbook. - **Real-world usage:** TLS 1.3 (ECDHE), SSH (DH), IPsec IKEv2, Signal Protocol (X3DH). - **Key takeaway for viva:** Be prepared to explain *why* $(g^a)^b = (g^b)^a$ but finding $a$ from $g^a$ is hard — this single sentence captures the essence of DHKE. ---

topics.title: Calculate the message digest of a text using the SHA-1 algorithm in JAVA.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGL508 - FOUNDATIONS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Calculate the message digest of a text using the SHA-1 algorithm in JAVA.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**SHA-1 (Secure Hash Algorithm – 1)** is a cryptographic hash function defined in **FIPS PUB 180-4** by the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST). It accepts an input message of any length and produces a fixed-size **160-bit (20-byte) message digest** (also called a hash, fingerprint, or checksum). The output is typically rendered as a **40-character hexadecimal string**.

The algorithm processes input in **512-bit (64-byte) blocks** using a Merkle–Damgård iterative construction and produces a hash composed of **five 32-bit words** ($H_0, H_1, H_2, H_3, H_4$) concatenated to form the final 160-bit digest.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Definition Box**
> *SHA-1 is a one-way, collision-resistant (in theory) cryptographic hash function that maps an arbitrary-length input to a 160-bit fixed-length output, computed in 80 rounds over padded 512-bit message blocks.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Unique Fingerprint" of Data

Imagine every human being on Earth has a **unique 20-character fingerprint code** assigned at birth. No two people share the same fingerprint, and you cannot reverse-engineer the person from the code alone.

Now replace "human" with **"digital file"** and "fingerprint" with **"160-bit hash"**:

- Every file — whether 1 KB or 10 GB — gets the **same size fingerprint** (40 hex characters).
- Change **one single byte** of the file → the **entire fingerprint changes** (avalanche effect).
- You **cannot reverse** the hash to recover the original file (one-way property).
- It is **computationally infeasible** to find two different files with the same fingerprint (collision resistance).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy used in KTU board exams:** *SHA-1 acts like a tamper-evident seal on a digital envelope. Anyone can verify whether the contents have been altered, but no one can reconstruct the contents from the seal itself.*

## 1.3 Core Technical Parameters (Bolded Constants)

| Parameter | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Hash Length** | **160 bits / 20 bytes / 40 hex chars** | Output digest size |
| **Block Size** | **512 bits / 64 bytes** | Input processed per round |
| **Word Size** | **32 bits** | Internal register width |
| **Rounds** | **80** | Total compression rounds per block |
| **Initial Hash (H0..H4)** | **0x67452301, 0xEFCDAB89, 0x98BADCFE, 0x10325476, 0xC3D2E1F0** | IV (RFC-defined magic numbers) |
| **Round Constants (K_t)** | **0x5A827999, 0x6ED9EBA1, 0x8F1BBCDC, 0xCA62C1D6** | Four 20-round phase constants |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Operational Phases of SHA-1

The algorithm executes in **five distinct phases** for every message input. A precise understanding of these phases is essential for both lab implementation and KTU theory questions.

### **Phase 1 — Padding the Message**
The original message of length $\ell$ bits is padded so its final length becomes a **multiple of 512 bits**.

**Procedure:**
1. Append a single **'1' bit** to the message.
2. Append **$k$ zero bits**, where $k$ is the smallest non-negative integer such that:
$$\ell + 1 + k \equiv 448 \pmod{512}$$
3. Append the **64-bit big-endian representation of $\ell$** (the original length in bits).

The final padded message is exactly a **multiple of 512 bits**.

### **Phase 2 — Initialize the Hash Buffers**
Five 32-bit registers are initialized with the magic constants:

$$H_0 = 0x67452301$$
$$H_1 = 0xEFCDAB89$$
$$H_2 = 0x98BADCFE$$
$$H_3 = 0x10325476$$
$$H_4 = 0xC3D2E1F0$$

### **Phase 3 — Message Schedule (Word Expansion)**
Each 512-bit block is split into 16 words $W_0 \dots W_{15}$ (each 32 bits). These are extended to **80 words** $W_0 \dots W_{79}$ using the recursive formula:

$$W_t = \text{ROTL}^1(W_{t-3} \oplus W_{t-8} \oplus W_{t-14} \oplus W_{t-16}) \quad \text{for } 16 \le t \le 79$$

where **ROTL$^n$** denotes a left circular rotation by $n$ bits.

### **Phase 4 — The 80 Compression Rounds**
The 80 rounds are divided into **four groups of 20**, each with its own **non-linear Boolean function $f_t$** and **constant $K_t$**:

| Round Group | Range of $t$ | Boolean Function $f_t(B,C,D)$ | Constant $K_t$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $0 \le t \le 19$ | $(B \land C) \lor (\lnot B \land D)$    *(Ch)* | **0x5A827999** |
| 2 | $20 \le t \le 39$ | $B \oplus C \oplus D$   *(Parity)* | **0x6ED9EBA1** |
| 3 | $40 \le t \le 59$ | $(B \land C) \lor (B \land D) \lor (C \land D)$   *(Maj)* | **0x8F1BBCDC** |
| 4 | $60 \le t \le 79$ | $B \oplus C \oplus D$   *(Parity)* | **0xCA62C1D6** |

Each round computes:
$$T = \text{ROTL}^5(A) + f_t(B,C,D) + E + K_t + W_t$$
$$E = D,\quad D = C,\quad C = \text{ROTL}^{30}(B),\quad B = A,\quad A = T$$

All additions are performed **modulo $2^{32}$**.

### **Phase 5 — Update Intermediate Hash**
After processing all 80 rounds of one block, the working variables are added to the hash buffers:
$$H_0 \leftarrow H_0 + A,\ H_1 \leftarrow H_1 + B,\ H_2 \leftarrow H_2 + C,\ H_3 \leftarrow H_3 + D,\ H_4 \leftarrow H_4 + E$$

The final 160-bit digest is the concatenation $H_0 \parallel H_1 \parallel H_2 \parallel H_3 \parallel H_4$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Value | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Padding condition | $\ell + 1 + k \equiv 448 \pmod{512}$ | Pre-processing |
| 2 | Word expansion | $W_t = \text{ROTL}^1(W_{t-3} \oplus W_{t-8} \oplus W_{t-14} \oplus W_{t-16})$ | 16→80 words |
| 3 | Round update | $T = \text{ROTL}^5(A) + f_t + E + K_t + W_t$ | Per-round computation |
| 4 | Function group 1 | $f_1 = (B \land C) \lor (\lnot B \land D)$ | Ch-function |
| 5 | Function group 2 & 4 | $f_2 = f_4 = B \oplus C \oplus D$ | Parity |
| 6 | Function group 3 | $f_3 = (B \land C) \lor (B \land D) \lor (C \land D)$ | Majority |
| 7 | $K_t$ constants | 0x5A827999, 0x6ED9EBA1, 0x8F1BBCDC, 0xCA62C1D6 | Round phase keys |
| 8 | Final digest | $\text{Digest} = H_0 \Vert H_1 \Vert H_2 \Vert H_3 \Vert H_4$ | 160-bit output |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic to remember the four $K_t$ values:**
> *"**5A**pple, **6E**lephant, **8F**rog, **CA**t"* — corresponding to 5A827999, 6ED9EBA1, 8F1BBCDC, CA62C1D6.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

SHA-1 (and its successors SHA-256/SHA-3) is the backbone of:
- **Digital signatures (DSA, RSA-PSS)** — sign the hash, not the file.
- **TLS/SSL handshake** — certificate fingerprinting (now deprecated for SHA-256).
- **Git version control** — every commit is identified by its SHA-1 hash.
- **File integrity verification** — checksums in software distribution.
- **Blockchain (legacy)** — older Bitcoin blocks used SHA-256 (a SHA-2 family member).
- **HMAC** — keyed-hash message authentication codes for API security.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Security Update (NEP 2020 context):** Although SHA-1 is still taught for academic understanding, real-world systems now prefer **SHA-256** because Google demonstrated a practical collision attack on SHA-1 in 2017 (the "SHAttered" attack, costing ~$110K).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — SHA-1 of the String `"abc"`

The string `"abc"` is the canonical KTU/FIPS test vector. We will manually trace **Padding** and the **first round** to make the algorithm tangible.

### **Step 1: Convert the Input to Binary**
ASCII codes: `a` = 0x61, `b` = 0x62, `c` = 0x63.

Binary representation (24 bits total):

$$
01100001\ 01100010\ 01100011
$$

So $\ell = 24$ bits.

### **Step 2: Apply Padding**
Append a single `1` bit → message length = 25 bits.

We need: $\ell + 1 + k \equiv 448 \pmod{512}$:

$$24 + 1 + k \equiv 448 \pmod{512} \implies k = 423 \text{ zero bits}$$

Append 64-bit big-endian representation of $\ell = 24 = 0x0000000000000018$:

$$
00000000\ 00000000\ 00000000\ 00000000\ 00000000\ 00000000\ 00000000\ 00011000
$$

**Final 512-bit padded block layout:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bits 0–23:}   & \quad 01100001\ 01100010\ 01100011 & & \text{(original "abc")} \\
\text{Bit 24:}      & \quad 1 & & \text{(mandatory 1-bit)} \\
\text{Bits 25–447:} & \quad 423 \text{ zero bits} & & \text{(padding)} \\
\text{Bits 448–511:} & \quad \text{64-bit length} = 0 \dots 00011000 & & \text{(message length)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 3: Parse the 512-bit Block into 16 Words (W0..W15)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_0  &= 0x61626380 & & \text{("abc" + first 8 bits of padding)} \\
W_1  &= 0x00000000 & & \\
W_2  &= 0x00000000 & & \\
W_3  &= 0x00000000 & & \\
W_4  &= 0x00000000 & & \\
W_5  &= 0x00000000 & & \\
W_6  &= 0x00000000 & & \\
W_7  &= 0x00000000 & & \\
W_8  &= 0x00000000 & & \\
W_9  &= 0x00000000 & & \\
W_{10} &= 0x00000000 & & \\
W_{11} &= 0x00000000 & & \\
W_{12} &= 0x00000000 & & \\
W_{13} &= 0x00000000 & & \\
W_{14} &= 0x00000000 & & \\
W_{15} &= 0x00000018 & & \text{(length in bits)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 4: Initialize Working Variables (A, B, C, D, E)**

For the first (and only) block, working variables are initialized from the IV:

$$
A = 0x67452301,\quad B = 0xEFCDAB89,\quad C = 0x98BADCFE,\quad D = 0x10325476,\quad E = 0xC3D2E1F0
$$

### **Step 5: First Round ($t = 0$, Group 1)**

Group 1 function: $f_0 = (B \land C) \lor (\lnot B \land D)$
Constant: $K_0 = 0x5A827999$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_0 &= (0xEFCDAB89 \land 0x98BADCFE) \lor (\lnot 0xEFCDAB89 \land 0x10325476) \\
    &= 0x88888888 \lor 0x00102246 \\
    &= 0x8898AACE
\end{aligned}
$$

The round update:

$$
T = \text{ROTL}^5(A) + f_0 + E + K_0 + W_0 \pmod{2^{32}}
$$

After performing **all 80 rounds** and **adding the final values to the IV**, the published RFC 3174 test vector yields:

$$
\text{SHA-1("abc")} = \texttt{a9993e364706816aba3e25717850c26c9cd0d89d}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** You are NOT required to manually compute all 80 rounds. KTU expects you to *show the padding step*, *state the round equations*, and *report the final hash value* (or the Java output).

## 3.2 Padding Worked Example — Longer Message

Suppose the message is `"Hello"` (5 bytes = 40 bits).
- Append `1` bit → 41 bits.
- Need $40 + 1 + k \equiv 448 \pmod{512}$, so $k = 407$ zero bits.
- Append 64-bit length = 0x0000000000000028.

Total padded length: $41 + 407 + 64 = 512$ bits ✓ — **exactly one 512-bit block**.

If the original message length $\ell$ satisfies $\ell \equiv 447 \pmod{512}$, padding requires an **extra full 512-bit block** to be appended. This is a frequently asked KTU viva question.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define SHA-1 and list its key properties.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**SHA-1 (Secure Hash Algorithm-1)** is a cryptographic hash function standardized by NIST in FIPS 180-4 that produces a **160-bit (40-character hexadecimal) message digest** from an input of arbitrary length.

**Key Properties (write any three for full marks):**
1. **Deterministic** — same input always produces the same output.
2. **Quick Computation** — the hash is computed efficiently in software/hardware.
3. **Pre-image Resistance** — given a hash $h$, it is infeasible to find $m$ such that $\text{Hash}(m) = h$.
4. **Avalanche Effect** — a 1-bit change in the input alters ~50% of the output bits.
5. **Collision Resistance** — computationally infeasible to find two distinct inputs $m_1 \ne m_2$ with the same hash (theoretically broken since 2017).

---

### **Q2. What is the block size and output size of SHA-1? Why is padding necessary?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Block size:** 512 bits (64 bytes).
- **Output size (hash length):** 160 bits (20 bytes) = 40 hexadecimal characters.
- **Padding is necessary** because SHA-1 processes the input as a sequence of fixed-size 512-bit blocks. The padding scheme (1 bit + zeros + 64-bit length) ensures that the **total length of the padded message is an exact multiple of 512 bits**, guaranteeing uniform block-wise processing. It also embeds the original message length to prevent length-extension attacks.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP — Pattern A]`

**Write a Java program to compute the message digest of a text using SHA-1. Explain the algorithm and verify with sample input "KTU 2024".**

#### **Part (a) — Algorithm Explanation (7 marks)**

The SHA-1 algorithm consists of **five steps**:

**Step 1 — Preprocessing (Padding):**
The original message of length $\ell$ bits is padded:
- Append a single '1' bit.
- Append $k$ zero bits so that $\ell + 1 + k \equiv 448 \pmod{512}$.
- Append the 64-bit big-endian binary representation of $\ell$.

Total padded length becomes a **multiple of 512 bits**.

**Step 2 — Initialize Hash Buffers:**
Five 32-bit registers are initialized:

$$
H_0 = 0x67452301,\ H_1 = 0xEFCDAB89,\ H_2 = 0x98BADCFE,\ H_3 = 0x10325476,\ H_4 = 0xC3D2E1F0
$$

**Step 3 — Message Schedule (Word Expansion):**
The 512-bit block is parsed into 16 words $W_0 \dots W_{15}$ and extended to 80 words:

$$
W_t = \text{ROTL}^1(W_{t-3} \oplus W_{t-8} \oplus W_{t-14} \oplus W_{t-16}),\ 16 \le t \le 79
$$

**Step 4 — Compression (80 Rounds in 4 Groups):**

| Group | $t$ Range | Function $f_t$ | $K_t$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|
| 1 | 0–19 | $(B \land C) \lor (\lnot B \land D)$ | 0x5A827999 |
| 2 | 20–39 | $B \oplus C \oplus D$ | 0x6ED9EBA1 |
| 3 | 40–59 | $(B \land C) \lor (B \land D) \lor (C \land D)$ | 0x8F1BBCDC |
| 4 | 60–79 | $B \oplus C \oplus D$ | 0xCA62C1D6 |

For each round:
$$T = \text{ROTL}^5(A) + f_t(B,C,D) + E + K_t + W_t \pmod{2^{32}}$$
$$E = D,\ D = C,\ C = \text{ROTL}^{30}(B),\ B = A,\ A = T$$

**Step 5 — Update Hash Buffers and Produce Digest:**
$$H_0 \mathrel{+}= A,\ H_1 \mathrel{+}= B,\ H_2 \mathrel{+}= C,\ H_3 \mathrel{+}= D,\ H_4 \mathrel{+}= E$$
$$\text{Digest} = H_0 \parallel H_1 \parallel H_2 \parallel H_3 \parallel H_4 \quad (160 \text{ bits})$$

#### **Part (b) — Java Implementation (7 marks)**

**Method 1 — Using Java's built-in `MessageDigest`:**

```java
import java.security.MessageDigest;
import java.security.NoSuchAlgorithmException;
import java.util.Scanner;

public class SHA1Digest {
    public static String bytesToHex(byte[] bytes) {
        StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
        for (byte b : bytes) {
            sb.append(String.format("%02x", b));
        }
        return sb.toString();
    }

    public static String computeSHA1(String input) throws NoSuchAlgorithmException {
        MessageDigest md = MessageDigest.getInstance("SHA-1");
        byte[] digest = md.digest(input.getBytes());
        return bytesToHex(digest);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter the text to hash: ");
        String message = sc.nextLine();

        try {
            String hexDigest = computeSHA1(message);
            System.out.println("Input Message : " + message);
            System.out.println("SHA-1 Digest  : " + hexDigest);
            System.out.println("Digest Length : " + (hexDigest.length() * 4) + " bits");
        } catch (NoSuchAlgorithmException e) {
            System.out.println("SHA-1 algorithm not available.");
        }
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Method 2 — Manual Implementation (Educational — shows the full algorithm):**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class SHA1Manual {
    private static final int[] H_INIT = {
        0x67452301, 0xEFCDAB89, 0x98BADCFE, 0x10325476, 0xC3D2E1F0
    };

    private static int rotl(int x, int n) {
        return ((x << n) | (x >>> (32 - n)));
    }

    private static String computeSHA1(byte[] message) {
        int originalLength = message.length;
        long bitLength = (long) originalLength * 8;

        // ---------- Phase 1: Padding ----------
        int padLength = (originalLength + 9 <= 64) ? 64 : ((originalLength + 9 + 63) / 64) * 64;
        byte[] padded = new byte[padLength];
        System.arraycopy(message, 0, padded, 0, originalLength);
        padded[originalLength] = (byte) 0x80;

        // Append 64-bit length in big-endian
        for (int i = 0; i < 8; i++) {
            padded[padLength - 1 - i] = (byte)(bitLength >>> (8 * i));
        }

        // ---------- Phase 2 & 3 & 4: Process each 512-bit block ----------
        int[] H = H_INIT.clone();
        int[] W = new int[80];

        for (int block = 0; block < padLength; block += 64) {
            // Parse 16 words
            for (int i = 0; i < 16; i++) {
                W[i] = ((padded[block + 4 * i]     & 0xFF) << 24) |
                       ((padded[block + 4 * i + 1] & 0xFF) << 16) |
                       ((padded[block + 4 * i + 2] & 0xFF) << 8)  |
                       ((padded[block + 4 * i + 3] & 0xFF));
            }
            // Word expansion to 80
            for (int i = 16; i < 80; i++) {
                W[i] = rotl(W[i-3] ^ W[i-8] ^ W[i-14] ^ W[i-16], 1);
            }

            int A = H[0], B = H[1], C = H[2], D = H[3], E = H[4];

            for (int t = 0; t < 80; t++) {
                int f, k;
                if (t < 20) {
                    f = (B & C) | ((~B) & D);
                    k = 0x5A827999;
                } else if (t < 40) {
                    f = B ^ C ^ D;
                    k = 0x6ED9EBA1;
                } else if (t < 60) {
                    f = (B & C) | (B & D) | (C & D);
                    k = 0x8F1BBCDC;
                } else {
                    f = B ^ C ^ D;
                    k = 0xCA62C1D6;
                }
                int T = rotl(A, 5) + f + E + k + W[t];
                E = D;
                D = C;
                C = rotl(B, 30);
                B = A;
                A = T;
            }

            H[0] += A; H[1] += B; H[2] += C; H[3] += D; H[4] += E;
        }

        // ---------- Phase 5: Construct hex digest ----------
        StringBuilder hex = new StringBuilder();
        for (int h : H) {
            hex.append(String.format("%08x", h));
        }
        return hex.toString();
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter the text to hash: ");
        String msg = sc.nextLine();
        String digest = computeSHA1(msg.getBytes());
        System.out.println("Input Message : " + msg);
        System.out.println("SHA-1 Digest  : " + digest);
        System.out.println("Length        : " + digest.length() + " hex chars = 160 bits");
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

#### **Part (b) — Java Program Demonstrating Avalanche Effect (7 marks)**

```java
import java.security.MessageDigest;
import java.security.NoSuchAlgorithmException;

public class SHA1AvalancheDemo {
    public static String sha1(String input) throws NoSuchAlgorithmException {
        MessageDigest md = MessageDigest.getInstance("SHA-1");
        byte[] digest = md.digest(input.getBytes());
        StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
        for (byte b : digest) {
            sb.append(String.format("%02X", b));
        }
        return sb.toString();
    }

    public static int countDifferingBits(String h1, String h2) {
        int diffs = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < h1.length(); i++) {
            int xor = Integer.parseInt(h1.substring(i, i+1), 16) ^
                      Integer.parseInt(h2.substring(i, i+1), 16);
            // count set bits in a hex digit
            if ((xor & 1) != 0) diffs++;
            if ((xor & 2) != 0) diffs++;
            if ((xor & 4) != 0) diffs++;
            if ((xor & 8) != 0) diffs++;
        }
        return diffs;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            String m1 = "Cryptography";
            String m2 = "cryptography";   // only first letter case changed

            String h1 = sha1(m1);
            String h2 = sha1(m2);

            System.out.println("Input 1 : " + m1);
            System.out.println("SHA-1 1 : " + h1);
            System.out.println("Input 2 : " + m2);
            System.out.println("SHA-1 2 : " + h2);
            System.out.println("Differing bits in hex: " + countDifferingBits(h1, h2) + " / 160");
            System.out.println("Avalanche % : " +
                String.format("%.2f", 100.0 * countDifferingBits(h1, h2) / 160.0) + "%");
        } catch (NoSuchAlgorithmException e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
    }
}
```
