## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ SHA-1 produces a **160-bit (20-byte, 40 hex-character)** message digest.
- ✅ The algorithm processes input in **512-bit (64-byte)** blocks.
- ✅ Internal computations use **five 32-bit working variables** $(A, B, C, D, E)$ and **five 32-bit hash registers** $(H_0 \dots H_4)$.
- ✅ The total number of compression rounds is **80**, split into **4 groups of 20**.
- ✅ The four round constants are **0x5A827999, 0x6ED9EBA1, 0x8F1BBCDC, 0xCA62C1D6**.
- ✅ Padding always adds a '1' bit, then zero bits, then a **64-bit big-endian length field**, making the total length a **multiple of 512 bits**.
- ✅ The four round functions are **Ch, Parity, Maj, Parity** (note: groups 2 and 4 share the same Parity function).
- ✅ SHA-1 exhibits the **avalanche effect** — ~50% of output bits change when a single input bit changes.
- ✅ SHA-1 is **collision-broken** (2017, Google "SHAttered"); for new applications, prefer **SHA-256** (FIPS 180-4).
- ✅ In Java, SHA-1 is available via `MessageDigest.getInstance("SHA-1")` — always mask bytes with `& 0xFF` to avoid signed-byte pitfalls.
- ✅ Standard KTU test vectors to memorize: `SHA-1("")` = `da39a3ee5e6b4b0d3255bfef95601890afd80709`, `SHA-1("abc")` = `a9993e364706816aba3e25717850c26c9cd0d89d`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a Java program to implement Diffie Hellman Key Exchange.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGL508 - FOUNDATIONS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a Java program to implement Diffie Hellman Key Exchange.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Diffie–Hellman Key Exchange (DHKE)** is a public-key cryptographic protocol that allows two communicating parties, who have no prior knowledge of each other, to jointly establish a **shared secret** over an **insecure communication channel**. The protocol was published in **1976** by Whitfield Diffie and Martin Hellman and is mathematically grounded in the computational intractability of the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)**.

Formally, given:
- A large public prime $p$ (the *modulus*)
- A public generator $g$ (a primitive root modulo $p$)
- Alice's private key $a$ and Bob's private key $b$

Both parties independently derive an identical shared secret:

$$K = g^{ab} \pmod{p}$$

The security of the system is anchored on the fact that while computing $g^{ab} \pmod{p}$ from the exchanged public values is easy, recovering the exponent $a$ (or $b$) from $g^{a} \pmod{p}$ is computationally **infeasible** for sufficiently large primes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Paint Mixing" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine Alice and Bob each start with a **common shared color of paint** (the public values $p$ and $g$). Alice secretly mixes her own private color into it and sends the result to Bob. Bob secretly mixes *his* own private color into the common paint and sends that to Alice. Now, Alice adds **her private color** to what Bob sent, and Bob adds **his private color** to what Alice sent. Both end up with the **same final mixed color** (the shared secret) — but a snooping eavesdropper Eve, who only sees the *intermediate* mixed colors, cannot easily reverse-engineer the private colors because unmixing paint is exponentially hard.

This asymmetry — *easy to mix, hard to unmix* — is the **discrete logarithm problem** at the heart of DH.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Parameters

| Parameter | Recommended Bit-Length | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Prime $p$ | **2048 bits** (NIST minimum) | Modulus; defines the group |
| Generator $g$ | Small integer (e.g., **2** or **5**) | Primitive root of $p$ |
| Private key $a, b$ | Same bit-length as $p$ | Random secret exponents |
| Shared secret $K$ | Same bit-length as $p$ | Symmetric key material |

> [!NOTE]
> The **RSA Laboratories** and **NIST SP 800-57** guidelines mandate a minimum **2048-bit** prime for DH to remain secure against modern discrete-log algorithms (Index Calculus, Number Field Sieve).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The Diffie–Hellman Key Exchange unfolds in a **symmetric 5-step flow**:

1. **Public Parameter Agreement**: Alice and Bob publicly agree on a large prime $p$ and a generator $g$, where $g$ is a primitive root modulo $p$.
2. **Private Key Generation**: Alice independently picks a random integer $a \in [2, p-2]$ as her private key. Bob independently picks $b \in [2, p-2]$ as his.
3. **Public Key Computation**:
   - Alice computes $A = g^{a} \pmod{p}$
   - Bob computes $B = g^{b} \pmod{p}$
4. **Public Key Exchange**: Alice sends $A$ to Bob; Bob sends $B$ to Alice (over the insecure channel).
5. **Shared Secret Derivation**:
   - Alice computes $K_{A} = B^{a} \pmod{p}$
   - Bob computes $K_{B} = A^{b} \pmod{p}$
   - By the laws of modular exponentiation, $K_{A} = K_{B} = g^{ab} \pmod{p}$.

## 2.2 Why the Keys Match — The 'Why' Behind the Math

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The correctness of DH relies on the **commutativity of modular exponentiation**:
> $$\left(g^{b}\right)^{a} \pmod{p} = g^{ba} \pmod{p} = g^{ab} \pmod{p} = \left(g^{a}\right)^{b} \pmod{p}$$
> This algebraic symmetry is what guarantees both parties arrive at the same shared secret without ever transmitting the secret itself.

## 2.3 The 'How' — Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)

The eavesdropper Eve sees $p$, $g$, $A$, and $B$. To recover the shared secret she would need to solve:
$$a = \log_{g}(A) \pmod{p}$$
This is the **Discrete Logarithm Problem**. For a 2048-bit prime, the fastest known algorithm (Number Field Sieve) requires sub-exponential but still **astronomical** time, making it infeasible in practice.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!WARNING]
> Per the KTU 2024 scheme, you must commit the following formulas to memory. Examiners routinely award full marks only when the **boundary conditions** (e.g., the range of $a, b$) and the **modular reduction operator** are explicitly written.

| # | Formula | Description | Notation / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = g^{a} \bmod p$ | Alice's public key | $A \in [1, p-1]$ |
| 2 | $B = g^{b} \bmod p$ | Bob's public key | $B \in [1, p-1]$ |
| 3 | $K_{A} = B^{a} \bmod p$ | Alice's derived secret | $K_{A} = g^{ab} \bmod p$ |
| 4 | $K_{B} = A^{b} \bmod p$ | Bob's derived secret | $K_{B} = g^{ab} \bmod p$ |
| 5 | $\left(g^{a}\right)^{b} \equiv \left(g^{b}\right)^{a} \pmod{p}$ | Commutativity law | Guarantees $K_{A} = K_{B}$ |
| 6 | $a = \log_{g} A \pmod{p}$ | Discrete log (Eve's hurdle) | Computationally infeasible |
| 7 | $a, b \in [2,\ p-2]$ | Private key valid range | Excludes trivial values $0, 1, p-1$ |
| 8 | $\text{Fermat: } a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ | Used in primitivity tests | $\gcd(a, p) = 1$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Diffie–Hellman (and its elliptic-curve variant **ECDH**) is the cornerstone of nearly every modern secure protocol:

- **TLS 1.3** handshake (used in HTTPS)
- **IPSec** VPN key establishment (IKEv2)
- **SSH** session key negotiation
- **Signal Protocol** (used by WhatsApp, Signal Messenger)
- **Wi-Fi WPA3** (SAE — Simultaneous Authentication of Equals)

> [!NOTE]
> **Forward Secrecy** in TLS 1.3 is achieved by generating **ephemeral DH keys** (DHE/ECDHE) for every session, ensuring that compromising one session key does not compromise past sessions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example (Small Prime)

Let us walk through DH using small numbers to make the algebra tangible.

> **Public Parameters (known to everyone):**
> - Prime modulus: $p = 23$
> - Generator: $g = 5$ (verified primitive root of 23)

**Step 1 — Alice picks a private key**

$$a = 6 \quad \text{(Alice's secret)}$$

**Step 2 — Alice computes her public key**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= g^{a} \bmod p \\
  &= 5^{6} \bmod 23 \\
  &= 15625 \bmod 23
\end{aligned}
$$

Reducing $15625 \div 23$ gives quotient $679$ and remainder $8$.

$$
A = 8
$$

**Step 3 — Bob picks a private key**

$$b = 15 \quad \text{(Bob's secret)}$$

**Step 4 — Bob computes his public key**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= g^{b} \bmod p \\
  &= 5^{15} \bmod 23
\end{aligned}
$$

We use repeated squaring for efficiency:
$$
\begin{aligned}
5^{1} \bmod 23 &= 5 \\
5^{2} \bmod 23 &= 25 \bmod 23 = 2 \\
5^{4} \bmod 23 &= 2^{2} \bmod 23 = 4 \\
5^{8} \bmod 23 &= 4^{2} \bmod 23 = 16 \\
5^{15} \bmod 23 &= 5^{8} \cdot 5^{4} \cdot 5^{2} \cdot 5^{1} \bmod 23 \\
                 &= 16 \cdot 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 \bmod 23 \\
                 &= 640 \bmod 23
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $23 \times 27 = 621$ and $640 - 621 = 19$:

$$B = 19$$

**Step 5 — Exchange & Derive Shared Secret**

Alice receives $B = 19$ and computes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{A} &= B^{a} \bmod p \\
      &= 19^{6} \bmod 23
\end{aligned}
$$

Repeated squaring:
$$
\begin{aligned}
19^{1} \bmod 23 &= 19 \\
19^{2} \bmod 23 &= 361 \bmod 23
\end{aligned}
$$

$23 \times 15 = 345$, so $361 - 345 = 16$. Thus $19^{2} \equiv 16 \pmod{23}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
19^{4} \bmod 23 &= 16^{2} \bmod 23 = 256 \bmod 23
\end{aligned}
$$

$23 \times 11 = 253$, so $256 - 253 = 3$. Thus $19^{4} \equiv 3 \pmod{23}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
19^{6} \bmod 23 &= 19^{4} \cdot 19^{2} \bmod 23 \\
                 &= 3 \cdot 16 \bmod 23 \\
                 &= 48 \bmod 23 \\
                 &= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$K_{A} = 2$$

**Verification by Bob:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{B} &= A^{b} \bmod p \\
      &= 8^{15} \bmod 23
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
8^{1} \bmod 23 &= 8 \\
8^{2} \bmod 23 &= 64 \bmod 23 = 18 \\
8^{4} \bmod 23 &= 18^{2} \bmod 23 = 324 \bmod 23
\end{aligned}
$$

$23 \times 14 = 322$, so $324 - 322 = 2$. Thus $8^{4} \equiv 2 \pmod{23}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
8^{8} \bmod 23 &= 2^{2} \bmod 23 = 4 \\
8^{15} \bmod 23 &= 8^{8} \cdot 8^{4} \cdot 8^{2} \cdot 8^{1} \bmod 23 \\
                 &= 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 18 \cdot 8 \bmod 23 \\
                 &= 1152 \bmod 23
\end{aligned}
$$

$23 \times 50 = 1150$, so $1152 - 1150 = 2$.

$$K_{B} = 2$$

> **✅ Match:** $K_{A} = K_{B} = 2$ — both parties now share the secret key **2** without ever transmitting it.

## 3.2 Complete Java Implementation (Lab-Ready Code)

The following is a publication-quality, lab-grade Java program that implements DH using the `java.math.BigInteger` class. `BigInteger` is **mandatory** because primitive `int` and `long` types overflow well before reaching cryptographic bit-lengths.

```java
import java.math.BigInteger;
import java.security.SecureRandom;

/**
 * DiffieHellmanKeyExchange.java
 * Lab Program: Implementation of the Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange Algorithm
 * KTU 2024 Scheme — PCCGL508 Foundations of Cryptography Lab
 */
public class DiffieHellmanKeyExchange {

    // Use a cryptographically strong random number generator
    private static final SecureRandom secureRandom = new SecureRandom();

    /**
     * Returns a probable prime of the given bit length.
     * ProbablePrime uses Miller-Rabin primality testing internally.
     */
    public static BigInteger generatePrime(int bitLength) {
        return BigInteger.probablePrime(bitLength, secureRandom);
    }

    /**
     * Returns a random BigInteger in the range [2, p-2] for use as a private key.
     */
    public static BigInteger generatePrivateKey(BigInteger p) {
        BigInteger privateKey;
        do {
            privateKey = new BigInteger(p.bitLength() - 1, secureRandom);
        } while (privateKey.compareTo(BigInteger.TWO) < 0
              || privateKey.compareTo(p.subtract(BigInteger.TWO)) > 0);
        return privateKey;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {

        System.out.println("=== Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange ===\n");

        // ---------- Step 1: Public Parameter Agreement ----------
        // Both parties agree on a large prime p and a generator g
        int bitLength = 512; // For demo; use 2048+ in production
        BigInteger p = generatePrime(bitLength);
        BigInteger g = BigInteger.valueOf(5); // Common generator

        System.out.println("Public Prime (p)   : " + p);
        System.out.println("Public Generator(g): " + g);

        // ---------- Step 2: Private Key Generation ----------
        BigInteger alicePrivate = generatePrivateKey(p);
        BigInteger bobPrivate   = generatePrivateKey(p);

        System.out.println("\nAlice's Private Key (a): " + alicePrivate);
        System.out.println("Bob's Private Key   (b): " + bobPrivate);

        // ---------- Step 3: Public Key Computation ----------
        // A = g^a mod p ;  B = g^b mod p
        BigInteger alicePublic = g.modPow(alicePrivate, p);
        BigInteger bobPublic   = g.modPow(bobPrivate,   p);

        System.out.println("\nAlice's Public Key  (A = g^a mod p): " + alicePublic);
        System.out.println("Bob's Public Key    (B = g^b mod p): " + bobPublic);

        // ---------- Step 4 & 5: Exchange & Derive Shared Secret ----------
        // Alice computes: K = B^a mod p
        // Bob   computes: K = A^b mod p
        // Both arrive at g^(ab) mod p
        BigInteger sharedSecretAlice = bobPublic.modPow(alicePrivate,   p);
        BigInteger sharedSecretBob   = alicePublic.modPow(bobPrivate,   p);

        System.out.println("\nShared Secret (Alice): " + sharedSecretAlice);
        System.out.println("Shared Secret (Bob)  : " + sharedSecretBob);

        // ---------- Verification ----------
        if (sharedSecretAlice.equals(sharedSecretBob)) {
            System.out.println("\n[SUCCESS] Shared secrets match!"
                             + " Key exchange is VALID.");
        } else {
            System.out.println("\n[FAILURE] Shared secrets DO NOT match.");
        }
    }
}
```

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Trace Table for the Code

| Step | Alice's State | Bob's State | Channel (Public) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Knows $p, g$ | Knows $p, g$ | $p = 23,\ g = 5$ (sample) |
| 2 | Picks $a$ secretly | Picks $b$ secretly | — (private) |
| 3 | Computes $A = g^{a} \bmod p$ | Computes $B = g^{b} \bmod p$ | — (private) |
| 4 | Sends $A$ | Sends $B$ | $A \rightarrow$ Bob, $B \rightarrow$ Alice |
| 5 | Computes $K = B^{a} \bmod p$ | Computes $K = A^{b} \bmod p$ | Both get $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In the Java code, we use `BigInteger.modPow(exponent, modulus)` which is optimized for modular exponentiation and internally applies the **Montgomery reduction** for huge integers — far superior to writing your own loop.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Protocol Flow — Alice and Bob Interaction

The following Mermaid sequence diagram captures the full round-trip of the Diffie–Hellman protocol, including the eavesdropper Eve who can only observe public values.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant Alice
    participant Bob
    participant Eve

    Note over Alice, Bob: Agreed Public Parameters: Prime p, Generator g
    Alice->>Alice: Pick private key a (secret)
    Bob->>Bob: Pick private key b (secret)

    Alice->>Alice: Compute A = g^a mod p
    Bob->>Bob: Compute B = g^b mod p

    Alice->>Bob: Send A (public)
    Note over Eve: Eavesdrops A
    Bob->>Alice: Send B (public)
    Note over Eve: Eavesdrops B

    Alice->>Alice: Compute Shared Secret = B^a mod p
    Bob->>Bob: Compute Shared Secret = A^b mod p

    Note over Alice, Bob: Both arrive at K = g^(ab) mod p
    Note over Eve: Cannot compute K without solving DLP
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture

This block diagram maps the cryptographic operations to their Java method abstractions, which is what the KTU lab viva expects you to draw on the record.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef pub fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0d47a1
    classDef sec fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#bf360c
    classDef out fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1b5e20

    Start([Start DH Key Exchange]):::pub
    ParamGen[Generate Public Prime p and Generator g]:::pub
    AlicePriv[Generate Alice Private Key a]:::sec
    BobPriv[Generate Bob Private Key b]:::sec
    AlicePub[Compute Alice Public Key A = g^a mod p]:::sec
    BobPub[Compute Bob Public Key B = g^b mod p]:::sec
    Exchange[Exchange A and B over Insecure Channel]:::pub
    AliceSecret[Alice computes K = B^a mod p]:::sec
    BobSecret[Bob computes K = A^b mod p]:::sec
    Match{Shared Secrets Match ?}:::pub
    Success[Output Shared Secret K]:::out
    Failure[Error: Keys Mismatch]:::out

    Start --> ParamGen
    ParamGen --> AlicePriv
    ParamGen --> BobPriv
    AlicePriv --> AlicePub
    BobPriv --> BobPub
    AlicePub --> Exchange
    BobPub --> Exchange
    Exchange --> AliceSecret
    Exchange --> BobSecret
    AliceSecret --> Match
    BobSecret --> Match
    Match -->|Yes| Success
    Match -->|No| Failure
```

## 4.3 Attack Surface Map (Eve's Perspective)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef seen fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#b71c1c
    classDef hidden fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1b5e20
    classDef solve fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#e65100

    p_g[p and g - Public Modulus and Generator]:::seen
    A_Pub[Alice Public Key A]:::seen
    B_Pub[Bob Public Key B]:::seen
    a_Priv[Alice Private Key a]:::hidden
    b_Priv[Bob Private Key b]:::hidden
    K_Shared[Shared Secret K]:::hidden
    DLP[Solve Discrete Logarithm Problem]:::solve

    p_g --> DLP
    A_Pub --> DLP
    B_Pub --> DLP
    DLP -.->|Infeasible for 2048+ bits| a_Priv
    DLP -.->|Infeasible for 2048+ bits| b_Priv
    DLP -.->|Cannot compute| K_Shared
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`
**Define the Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange algorithm. List any two applications.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 Marks):** Diffie–Hellman Key Exchange is a public-key cryptographic protocol that enables two parties to establish a shared secret over an insecure channel. It is based on the computational intractability of the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)**.
>
> **Applications (1 Mark, any two):**
> 1. **TLS/SSL** handshake for HTTPS (specifically DHE/ECDHE cipher suites).
> 2. **IPSec / VPN** key negotiation via IKEv2.
> 3. **SSH** session key establishment.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is the Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP) and why is it central to the security of Diffie-Hellman?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 Marks):** Given $p$, $g$, and $y = g^{x} \bmod p$, the Discrete Logarithm Problem is the task of finding the integer $x$. It is considered computationally infeasible for primes larger than **2048 bits**.
>
> **Relevance to DH (1 Mark):** An eavesdropper sees $A = g^{a} \bmod p$ and $B = g^{b} \bmod p$, but cannot compute $a$ or $b$ (or directly $g^{ab} \bmod p$) because doing so requires solving the DLP — a task believed to be intractable in polynomial time.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange algorithm with a neat block diagram. Describe each step with the corresponding mathematical expressions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two users Alice and Bob agree on a prime $p = 17$ and a generator $g = 3$. Alice chooses $a = 5$ and Bob chooses $b = 7$. Compute the shared secret key. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Algorithm Explanation (7 Marks):**

1. **Public Parameter Agreement (1 Mark):** Alice and Bob agree on a large prime $p$ and a generator $g$ (primitive root modulo $p$).
2. **Private Key Generation (1 Mark):** Alice picks $a$ secretly; Bob picks $b$ secretly, where $a, b \in [2, p-2]$.
3. **Public Key Computation (2 Marks):**
   - Alice computes: $A = g^{a} \bmod p$
   - Bob computes: $B = g^{b} \bmod p$
4. **Exchange (1 Mark):** Alice and Bob exchange $A$ and $B$ over the public channel.
5. **Shared Secret Derivation (1 Mark):**
   - Alice: $K_{A} = B^{a} \bmod p$
   - Bob: $K_{B} = A^{b} \bmod p$
6. **Verification (1 Mark):** $K_{A} = K_{B} = g^{ab} \bmod p$ by the commutativity of modular exponentiation.

A block diagram was drawn as in **Section 4.2** above.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

Given: $p = 17$, $g = 3$, $a = 5$, $b = 7$.

**Alice's Public Key:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= g^{a} \bmod p \\
  &= 3^{5} \bmod 17 \\
  &= 243 \bmod 17
\end{aligned}
$$

$17 \times 14 = 238$, so $243 - 238 = 5$.

$$A = 5$$

**Bob's Public Key:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= g^{b} \bmod p \\
  &= 3^{7} \bmod 17
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
3^{2} \bmod 17 &= 9 \\
3^{4} \bmod 17 &= 9^{2} \bmod 17 = 81 \bmod 17
\end{aligned}
$$

$17 \times 4 = 68$, so $81 - 68 = 13$. Thus $3^{4} \equiv 13 \pmod{17}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
3^{7} \bmod 17 &= 3^{4} \cdot 3^{2} \cdot 3^{1} \bmod 17 \\
                &= 13 \cdot 9 \cdot 3 \bmod 17 \\
                &= 351 \bmod 17
\end{aligned}
$$

$17 \times 20 = 340$, so $351 - 340 = 11$.

$$B = 11$$

**Shared Secret (Alice's side):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{A} &= B^{a} \bmod p \\
      &= 11^{5} \bmod 17
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
11^{2} \bmod 17 &= 121 \bmod 17
\end{aligned}
$$

$17 \times 7 = 119$, so $121 - 119 = 2$. Thus $11^{2} \equiv 2 \pmod{17}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
11^{4} \bmod 17 &= 2^{2} \bmod 17 = 4 \\
11^{5} \bmod 17 &= 11^{4} \cdot 11^{1} \bmod 17 = 4 \cdot 11 \bmod 17 = 44 \bmod 17
\end{aligned}
$$

$17 \times 2 = 34$, so $44 - 34 = 10$.

$$K_{A} = 10$$

**Verification (Bob's side):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{B} &= A^{b} \bmod p \\
      &= 5^{7} \bmod 17
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
5^{2} \bmod 17 &= 25 \bmod 17 = 8 \\
5^{4} \bmod 17 &= 8^{2} \bmod 17 = 64 \bmod 17
\end{aligned}
$$

$17 \times 3 = 51$, so $64 - 51 = 13$. Thus $5^{4} \equiv 13 \pmod{17}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
5^{7} \bmod 17 &= 5^{4} \cdot 5^{2} \cdot 5^{1} \bmod 17 \\
                &= 13 \cdot 8 \cdot 5 \bmod 17 \\
                &= 520 \bmod 17
\end{aligned}
$$

$17 \times 30 = 510$, so $520 - 510 = 10$.

$$K_{B} = 10$$

> **✅ Shared Secret = 10**, verified by both parties.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Alternative Set]`

**(a)** Discuss the security of the Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange algorithm. Explain the role of the Discrete Logarithm Problem. What is a Man-in-the-Middle attack and how can it be prevented? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to implement the Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange algorithm using `BigInteger` and `SecureRandom`. Briefly explain the role of `modPow()`. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Security Analysis (7 Marks):**

1. **Discrete Logarithm Problem (2 Marks):** The security of DH rests on the fact that, given $p$, $g$, and $g^{x} \bmod p$, recovering $x$ is computationally infeasible for sufficiently large primes. This is the DLP. Modern algorithms (Number Field Sieve) have sub-exponential complexity but remain intractable for 2048+ bit primes.
2. **Why the Secret is Safe (1 Mark):** Even if Eve captures $A$ and $B$, she cannot directly compute $g^{ab} \bmod p$ without first solving the DLP for one of the private keys.
3. **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) Attack (2 Marks):** An active attacker Mallory can intercept $A$ and $B$, replace them with her own public keys $A'$ and $B'$, and establish two separate shared secrets — one with Alice and one with Bob. Alice and Bob believe they are talking to each other, but Mallory decrypts, reads, and re-encrypts all traffic.
4. **MITM Prevention (2 Marks):**
   - **Authentication of public keys** via **digital signatures** (e.g., RSA or ECDSA certificates).
   - Use of **Trusted Certificates** (X.509 PKI in TLS).
   - **STS (Station-to-Station) protocol** where each party signs the exchanged DH values.

**(b) Java Program (7 Marks):**

Refer to the complete Java program in **Section 3.2** above. The structure of the answer should include:

1. **Import statements** for `BigInteger` and `SecureRandom` (0.5 Marks).
2. **Prime generation** using `BigInteger.probablePrime(bitLength, random)` (1.5 Marks).
3. **Private key generation** as a random BigInteger in the valid range (1 Mark).
4. **Public key computation** using `g.modPow(a, p)` (2 Marks).
5. **Shared secret derivation** and equality check (1.5 Marks).
6. **Explanation of `modPow()` (0.5 Marks):** `modPow(exponent, modulus)` computes $base^{exponent} \bmod modulus$ efficiently using fast modular exponentiation (square-and-multiply with Montgomery reduction), avoiding intermediate BigInteger overflow and keeping the computation tractable for 2048-bit primes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the modular reduction** in derivations: writing $A = g^{a}$ instead of $A = g^{a} \bmod p$. The `mod p` is non-negotiable. (-1 to -2 marks)
> 2. **Using primitive `int` or `long`** in the Java code: this overflows for primes > 63 bits. Always use `BigInteger`. (-2 marks)
> 3. **Not verifying the key match**: a program that prints the two secrets but does not compare them is incomplete. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Confusing DH with RSA**: DH is a *key exchange* protocol, not an encryption algorithm. The shared secret is then used with a symmetric cipher (e.g., AES). (-2 marks)
> 5. **Not discussing the MITM vulnerability**: DH alone is unauthenticated. Always mention authentication in the viva. (-1 mark)
> 6. **Off-by-one in private key range**: private keys must lie in $[2, p-2]$, not $[1, p-1]$. The values $0, 1, p-1$ yield trivial or predictable secrets. (-1 mark)

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Diffie–Hellman Key Exchange**

- ✅ **DH is a key-exchange protocol, not encryption.** It establishes a *shared secret* used later with symmetric ciphers.
- ✅ **Five steps:** Agree $(p, g)$ → Pick private keys → Compute public keys → Exchange → Derive shared secret.
- ✅ **Core formulas:**
  - $A = g^{a} \bmod p$, $B = g^{b} \bmod p$
  - $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$ (both parties)
- ✅ **Commutativity** of modular exponentiation guarantees $K_{A} = K_{B}$.
- ✅ **Security rests on DLP**: recovering $a$ from $g^{a} \bmod p$ is infeasible for $p \geq 2048$ bits.
- ✅ **MITM vulnerability**: DH alone is *unauthenticated*; must be combined with **digital signatures** or **certificates**.
- ✅ **In Java, always use `BigInteger` and `SecureRandom`** — never primitive types for cryptography.
- ✅ **`modPow(exp, mod)`** is the canonical method for modular exponentiation; it is fast and overflow-safe.
- ✅ **Private key range**: $a, b \in [2, p-2]$ to avoid trivial values.
- ✅ **Real-world protocols using DH**: TLS 1.3 (DHE/ECDHE), IPSec, SSH, Signal, WPA3-SAE.
- ✅ **For lab record**: include algorithm steps, the Java code, sample input/output, and a screenshot of the console matching keys.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of AES

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Advanced Encryption Standard (AES)** is a symmetric-key block cipher standardized by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** in **FIPS PUB 197** (November 2001). It operates on **128-bit (16-byte) data blocks** and supports three standardized key lengths: **128, 192, or 256 bits**. The algorithm is based on the **Rijndael cipher** designed by cryptographers **Vincent Rijmen** and **Joan Daemen** of Belgium. AES performs a fixed number of transformation rounds — **10 rounds for AES-128**, **12 rounds for AES-192**, and **14 rounds for AES-256** — over a 4×4 column-major order matrix of bytes called the **State**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** AES is the only publicly accessible symmetric cipher approved by NIST for protecting **Top Secret** classified information when used with 192 or 256-bit keys (NSA Suite B Cryptography).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sealed Box" Intuition

Imagine you want to send a precious necklace through the postal system. Instead of mailing it openly, you place it inside a **locked steel box with a numeric keypad**. The postal workers (analogous to a potential attacker) can pick up the box, shake it, even try to pry it — but the necklace remains inaccessible without the correct code.

- **The box** = Your 128-bit plaintext block.
- **The numeric keypad** = Your secret key (128/192/256 bits).
- **Picking the lock through brute force** = Trying all $2^{128}$ or more possible key combinations.
- **Multiple nested layers of the box** = The 10/12/14 rounds of transformation.

The number of possible key combinations for **AES-128** is:

$$2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$$

Even if every computer on Earth (about **10 billion** devices) tried **1 billion keys per second**, it would still take longer than the age of the universe (~13.8 billion years) to exhaust the key space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Advanced"?** AES replaced the aging **Data Encryption Standard (DES)** in 2001 because DES's 56-bit key was brute-forced in under 22 hours by the EFF "Deep Crack" machine in 1999. AES was selected through an open, transparent international competition — a model rarely used in cryptography standardization.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Block size | **128 bits (16 bytes)** |
| Key sizes | **128 / 192 / 256 bits** |
| Rounds (Nr) | **10 / 12 / 14** |
| State matrix | **4 × 4 bytes** |
| Word size | **32 bits (4 bytes)** |
| Key schedule words | **Nb(Nr+1) = 44 / 52 / 60** |
| Origin | **Rijndael (Belgium, 1998)** |
| Standard | **FIPS PUB 197 (2001)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Fundamental AES Round Transformations

Every AES round (except the final round) executes four distinct, reversible operations on the **State** matrix. Each step provides **confusion** or **diffusion** — the two cornerstones of Claude Shannon's information-theoretic security.

### 2.1.1 SubBytes (Confusion Layer)
- A non-linear byte substitution where each byte of the State is replaced independently using a fixed **16 × 16 lookup table** known as the **S-Box (Substitution Box)**.
- The S-Box is constructed by taking the **multiplicative inverse in the Galois Field GF($2^8$)** and then applying an **affine transformation** over GF(2).
- Provides **confusion** — the relationship between key and ciphertext is obscured.

### 2.1.2 ShiftRows (Diffusion Layer)
- The State rows are cyclically shifted to the left by **0, 1, 2, and 3 byte positions** respectively for rows 0, 1, 2, and 3.
- Row 0 is unchanged; Row 1 shifts by 1; Row 2 by 2; Row 3 by 3.
- Provides **diffusion** — bits of a single plaintext byte influence multiple ciphertext bytes.

### 2.1.3 MixColumns (Diffusion Layer)
- Each column of the State is treated as a 4-term polynomial over **GF($2^8$)** and multiplied modulo $x^4 + 1$ with a fixed polynomial:

$$a(x) = \{03\}x^3 + \{01\}x^2 + \{01\}x + \{02\}$$

- The byte values $\{02\}$ and $\{03\}$ represent the polynomials $x$ and $x+1$ over GF($2^8$).
- This is a linear operation ensuring that each output byte depends on **all four input bytes** of the column.

### 2.1.4 AddRoundKey (Key Mixing)
- A simple **XOR (bitwise exclusive-OR)** of the State with a 128-bit **round key** derived from the original cipher key through the **Key Expansion** routine.
- This is the only step that uses the key material in each round.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The final round (round Nr)** omits the **MixColumns** operation. This is by design: skipping MixColumns in the last round makes the **decryption algorithm's structure symmetric** with encryption, allowing the same key schedule to be used in reverse.

## 2.2 The AES Key Expansion Algorithm

The original key is expanded into **Nb(Nr+1) = 44, 52, or 60** 32-bit words (depending on key size). The expansion uses:

- **RotWord** — cyclic left rotation of a 4-byte word by one byte.
- **SubWord** — S-Box substitution applied to each of the 4 bytes.
- **Rcon (Round Constant)** — a word whose bytes are powers of $\{02\}$ in GF($2^8$): $\text{Rcon}[i] = \{02\}^{i-1}, \{00\}, \{00\}, \{00\}$.

The recurrence relation is:

$$W[i] = \begin{cases} K[i] & \text{if } i < N_k \\ W[i-N_k] \oplus \text{SubWord}(\text{RotWord}(W[i-1])) \oplus \text{Rcon}[i/N_k] & \text{if } i \geq N_k \text{ and } i \equiv 0 \pmod{N_k} \\ W[i-N_k] \oplus \text{SubWord}(W[i-1]) & \text{if } i \geq N_k, N_k = 8, \text{ and } i \equiv 4 \pmod{N_k} \\ W[i-N_k] \oplus W[i-1] & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical / Operational Expression | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|
| Block size | $128 \text{ bits} = 16 \text{ bytes} = 4 \times 4 \text{ matrix}$ | Fixed in all AES variants |
| Key space | $2^{128}$, $2^{192}$, or $2^{256}$ | Resistance to brute-force |
| Number of rounds | $N_r = 10$ (128), $12$ (192), $14$ (256) | $\text{Key Length (bits)} / 32 + 6$ |
| MixColumns polynomial | $a(x) = \{03\}x^3 + \{01\}x^2 + \{01\}x + \{02\}$ | Multiplied mod $x^4+1$ |
| GF($2^8$) reduction | If result $\geq 256$: XOR with $\{1B\}$ (= $0x11B$) | Field reduction constant |
| AddRoundKey | $S_{\text{out}}[i,j] = S_{\text{in}}[i,j] \oplus K_{\text{round}}[i,j]$ | Bitwise XOR with round key |
| ShiftRows | Row $r$ shifts left by $r$ positions | Cyclic byte rotation |
| S-Box construction | $b'_i = b_i \oplus b_{(i+4) \bmod 8} \oplus b_{(i+5) \bmod 8} \oplus b_{(i+6) \bmod 8} \oplus b_{(i+7) \bmod 8} \oplus c_i$ | Affine transform after GF inverse |
| Cipher operation | $C = E_K(P)$ where $E_K$ is the AES permutation | Symmetric encryption |
| Inverse cipher | $P = D_K(C) = E_K^{-1}(C)$ with **InvSubBytes, InvShiftRows, InvMixColumns, AddRoundKey** | Decryption |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Applications:** AES secures HTTPS/TLS, Wi-Fi (WPA2/WPA3), full-disk encryption (BitLocker, FileVault), VPNs, SSH, cryptocurrency wallets, and government/military communications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Java Implementation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Java Cryptography Architecture (JCA) — The Required Toolkits

Java provides the **Java Cryptography Architecture (JCA)** and the **Java Cryptography Extension (JCE)** as part of `javax.crypto` and `java.security` packages. The mandatory classes for implementing AES in a lab examination are:

| Class | Package | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `javax.crypto.Cipher` | `javax.crypto` | Provides the encryption/decryption engine |
| `javax.crypto.KeyGenerator` | `javax.crypto` | Programmatically generates secret keys |
| `javax.crypto.SecretKey` | `javax.crypto` | Represents the symmetric secret key |
| `javax.crypto.spec.SecretKeySpec` | `javax.crypto.spec` | Builds a key from raw byte array |
| `java.security.SecureRandom` | `java.security` | Cryptographically strong RNG |
| `java.util.Base64` | `java.util` | Encodes binary data to text for display |

## 3.2 Two Accepted Implementation Approaches in KTU Labs

### Approach A — Using `KeyGenerator` (dynamic key generation)

```java
import javax.crypto.Cipher;
import javax.crypto.KeyGenerator;
import javax.crypto.SecretKey;
import java.util.Base64;

public class AESExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        // Step 1: Generate a 128-bit AES key
        KeyGenerator keyGen = KeyGenerator.getInstance("AES");
        keyGen.init(128); // can be 128, 192, or 256
        SecretKey secretKey = keyGen.generateKey();

        // Step 2: Define the plaintext
        String plainText = "KTU Cryptography Lab - AES Algorithm";
        byte[] plainBytes = plainText.getBytes("UTF-8");

        // Step 3: Initialize the Cipher for encryption
        Cipher cipher = Cipher.getInstance("AES/ECB/PKCS5Padding");
        cipher.init(Cipher.ENCRYPT_MODE, secretKey);
        byte[] cipherBytes = cipher.doFinal(plainBytes);

        // Step 4: Display ciphertext in Base64
        String cipherText = Base64.getEncoder().encodeToString(cipherBytes);
        System.out.println("Plaintext  : " + plainText);
        System.out.println("Ciphertext : " + cipherText);

        // Step 5: Decrypt the ciphertext
        cipher.init(Cipher.DECRYPT_MODE, secretKey);
        byte[] decryptedBytes = cipher.doFinal(cipherBytes);
        String decryptedText = new String(decryptedBytes, "UTF-8");
        System.out.println("Decrypted  : " + decryptedText);
    }
}
```

### Approach B — Using `SecretKeySpec` (user-supplied key) — *Most Common in KTU Lab Exams*

```java
import javax.crypto.Cipher;
import javax.crypto.spec.SecretKeySpec;
import java.util.Base64;
import java.util.Scanner;

public class AESUserKey {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);

        // Step 1: Accept 16-byte (128-bit) key from the user
        System.out.print("Enter 16-character secret key: ");
        String keyString = sc.nextLine();
        byte[] keyBytes = keyString.getBytes("UTF-8");
        SecretKeySpec secretKey = new SecretKeySpec(keyBytes, "AES");

        // Step 2: Accept the plaintext
        System.out.print("Enter plaintext message      : ");
        String plainText = sc.nextLine();
        byte[] plainBytes = plainText.getBytes("UTF-8");

        // Step 3: Encrypt with AES in CBC mode with PKCS5 padding
        Cipher encryptCipher = Cipher.getInstance("AES/CBC/PKCS5Padding");
        byte[] iv = new byte[16]; // Initialization Vector (all zeros for demo)
        javax.crypto.spec.IvParameterSpec ivSpec =
                new javax.crypto.spec.IvParameterSpec(iv);
        encryptCipher.init(Cipher.ENCRYPT_MODE, secretKey, ivSpec);
        byte[] cipherBytes = encryptCipher.doFinal(plainBytes);

        String cipherText = Base64.getEncoder().encodeToString(cipherBytes);
        System.out.println("\nEncrypted (Base64): " + cipherText);

        // Step 4: Decrypt to verify round-trip correctness
        Cipher decryptCipher = Cipher.getInstance("AES/CBC/PKCS5Padding");
        decryptCipher.init(Cipher.DECRYPT_MODE, secretKey, ivSpec);
        byte[] decryptedBytes = decryptCipher.doFinal(cipherBytes);
        String decryptedText = new String(decryptedBytes, "UTF-8");
        System.out.println("Decrypted        : " + decryptedText);

        sc.close();
    }
}
```

## 3.3 Exhaustive Line-by-Line Analysis (Approach A Walkthrough)

| Line | Code | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `import javax.crypto.Cipher;` | Imports the cryptographic engine that performs the actual AES transformation. |
| 2 | `import javax.crypto.KeyGenerator;` | Imports the factory that creates cryptographically strong secret keys. |
| 3 | `import javax.crypto.SecretKey;` | Represents an opaque symmetric key (the AES key object). |
| 4 | `import java.util.Base64;` | Encodes binary ciphertext into printable ASCII so it can be displayed or transmitted safely. |
| 5 | `KeyGenerator keyGen = KeyGenerator.getInstance("AES");` | Requests an `AES`-specific `KeyGenerator` from the JCA provider. |
| 6 | `keyGen.init(128);` | Sets the key length to **128 bits**. Other valid values: **192, 256**. |
| 7 | `SecretKey secretKey = keyGen.generateKey();` | Generates a fresh, random AES key using the platform's `SecureRandom`. |
| 8 | `String plainText = "KTU Cryptography Lab - AES Algorithm";` | The original unencrypted message. |
| 9 | `byte[] plainBytes = plainText.getBytes("UTF-8");` | Converts the `String` into a UTF-8 encoded byte array (raw input format for AES). |
| 10 | `Cipher cipher = Cipher.getInstance("AES/ECB/PKCS5Padding");` | Creates a Cipher instance. **ECB** is the simplest mode; **PKCS5Padding** ensures plaintext length is a multiple of 16 bytes. |
| 11 | `cipher.init(Cipher.ENCRYPT_MODE, secretKey);` | Configures the Cipher for encryption using the generated secret key. |
| 12 | `byte[] cipherBytes = cipher.doFinal(plainBytes);` | Executes the AES algorithm. Returns the encrypted byte array (ciphertext). |
| 13 | `String cipherText = Base64.getEncoder().encodeToString(cipherBytes);` | Encodes binary ciphertext into Base64 for human-readable display. |
| 14 | `cipher.init(Cipher.DECRYPT_MODE, secretKey);` | Re-initializes the same Cipher object for decryption. **Same key** must be reused. |
| 15 | `byte[] decryptedBytes = cipher.doFinal(cipherBytes);` | Performs AES decryption; output should equal the original plaintext bytes. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for KTU Viva:** In `Cipher.getInstance(...)`, the format is `"Algorithm/Mode/Padding"`. Java supports `"AES"`, `"AES/ECB/PKCS5Padding"`, `"AES/CBC/PKCS5Padding"`, `"AES/GCM/NoPadding"`, etc. **ECB mode is the least secure** because identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks. Real-world systems use **CBC, CTR, or GCM**.

## 3.4 Modes of Operation — Why They Matter

| Mode | Full Name | IV Required? | Security | Parallelizable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ECB** | Electronic Codebook | No | ❌ Insecure for patterns | ✅ Yes |
| **CBC** | Cipher Block Chaining | Yes (16 bytes) | ✅ Standard | ❌ Decrypt only |
| **CFB** | Cipher Feedback | Yes | ✅ | ❌ |
| **OFB** | Output Feedback | Yes | ✅ | ❌ |
| **CTR** | Counter | Yes (nonce) | ✅ Recommended | ✅ Both |
| **GCM** | Galois/Counter Mode | Yes (12 bytes) | ✅✅ Authenticated encryption | ✅ Both |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked to "implement AES with better security", always pick **CBC mode with PKCS5Padding** and supply a non-zero, random **Initialization Vector (IV)**. Modern best practice is **AES-GCM** which provides both **confidentiality and integrity**.

## 3.5 Expected Output (Sample Run)

```
Plaintext  : KTU Cryptography Lab - AES Algorithm
Ciphertext : 8r2QvFp9K3xYbZ1mN+qW4TcV7sR0eH2kKj5Ld6Mg8Pw=
Decrypted  : KTU Cryptography Lab - AES Algorithm
```

Note that the **ciphertext is unreadable gibberish** and the **decrypted text exactly matches the original plaintext** — this round-trip equality proves the AES implementation is correct.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AES Encryption Round Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input: 128-bit Plaintext Block"] --> B["Initial AddRoundKey with Key[0..3]"]
    B --> C1["Round 1: SubBytes"]
    C1 --> C2["Round 1: ShiftRows"]
    C2 --> C3["Round 1: MixColumns"]
    C3 --> C4["Round 1: AddRoundKey with Key[4..7]"]
    C4 --> D1["Round 2: SubBytes"]
    D1 --> D2["Round 2: ShiftRows"]
    D2 --> D3["Round 2: MixColumns"]
    D3 --> D4["Round 2: AddRoundKey with Key[8..11]"]
    D4 --> E1["..."]
    E1 --> F1["Final Round: SubBytes"]
    F1 --> F2["Final Round: ShiftRows"]
    F2 --> F3["Final Round: AddRoundKey"]
    F3 --> G["Output: 128-bit Ciphertext Block"]
```

## 4.2 AES Single-Round Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Plaintext State 4x4"] --> SB["SubBytes: S-Box Lookup"]
    SB --> SR["ShiftRows: Cyclic Left Shift"]
    SR --> MC["MixColumns: GF(2^8) Matrix Multiply"]
    MC --> AK["AddRoundKey: XOR with Round Key"]
    AK --> OUT["New State Output"]
```

## 4.3 Java AES Program Execution Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U["User Input: Plaintext and Key"] --> KG["KeyGenerator or SecretKeySpec"]
    KG --> SK["SecretKey Object"]
    SK --> CI["Cipher.getInstance(AES Algorithm)"]
    U --> PB["Plaintext Byte Array"]
    PB --> CI
    CI --> ENC["cipher.init(ENCRYPT_MODE)"]
    ENC --> ENC2["cipher.doFinal(plainBytes)"]
    ENC2 --> CT["Ciphertext Byte Array"]
    CT --> B64["Base64 Encoding"]
    B64 --> DISP["Display Ciphertext"]
    CT --> DEC["cipher.init(DECRYPT_MODE)"]
    DEC --> DEC2["cipher.doFinal(cipherBytes)"]
    DEC2 --> PT["Decrypted Byte Array"]
    PT --> OUT["Display Recovered Plaintext"]
```

## 4.4 AES Key Expansion Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K0["Original Cipher Key Nk Words"] --> TEST{"i divisible by Nk?"}
    TEST -- "Yes" --> ROT["RotWord: rotate 4 bytes left"]
    ROT --> SUB["SubWord: apply S-Box to each byte"]
    SUB --> RC["XOR with Rcon i/Nk"]
    RC --> W_NEW["Generate W[i]"]
    TEST -- "No" --> W_DIRECT["W[i] = W[i-Nk] XOR W[i-1]"]
    W_DIRECT --> W_NEW
    K0 --> W_NEW
    W_NEW --> EXP["Expanded Key Schedule: Nb times Nr+1 words"]
    EXP --> RK1["Round Key for Round 1"]
    EXP --> RK2["Round Key for Round 2"]
    EXP --> RKN["Round Key for Final Round"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`
**Q: List any three differences between AES and DES algorithms.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | DES (Data Encryption Standard) | AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Block size: **64 bits** | Block size: **128 bits** |
| 2 | Key size: **56 bits** | Key size: **128, 192, or 256 bits** |
| 3 | Based on **Feistel network** | Based on **Substitution-Permutation Network** |
| 4 | Number of rounds: **16** | Number of rounds: **10, 12, or 14** |
| 5 | Designed by **IBM (1977)** | Designed by **Rijmen & Daemen (2001)** |
| 6 | Vulnerable to **brute force** (broken in 1999) | Considered **computationally secure** |

Any three valid points earn full marks.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**Q: What is the role of the S-Box in the AES SubBytes step?**

**Model Answer:**
The **S-Box (Substitution Box)** in AES SubBytes is a fixed 16 × 16 lookup table containing byte values from 0x00 to 0xFF. It is constructed by:
1. Computing the **multiplicative inverse** of each byte in the **Galois Field GF($2^8$)**, with 0x00 mapped to itself.
2. Applying a fixed **affine transformation** over GF(2) to the inverse.

The S-Box introduces **non-linearity** (confusion) into the cipher, ensuring that simple algebraic relationships between the key and ciphertext cannot be exploited. Without the S-Box, AES would reduce to a linear system solvable by Gaussian elimination.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q (a)** Explain the AES algorithm with a neat block diagram. Discuss each of the four transformations used in a single round. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**AES Algorithm Overview:**
The Advanced Encryption Standard operates on a 128-bit data block organized as a 4×4 byte matrix called the **State**. For **AES-128**, the algorithm performs **10 rounds** of transformation: an initial round (AddRoundKey only), 9 standard rounds (SubBytes → ShiftRows → MixColumns → AddRoundKey), and a final round (SubBytes → ShiftRows → AddRoundKey, **no MixColumns**).

**The Four Round Transformations:**

1. **SubBytes:** Each byte in the State is replaced by its corresponding value in a 16×16 S-Box. This step provides **confusion** by making the relationship between key and ciphertext non-linear.

2. **ShiftRows:** Rows 1, 2, and 3 of the State are cyclically shifted left by 1, 2, and 3 byte positions respectively; Row 0 is unchanged. This provides **diffusion** by spreading byte influence across the block.

3. **MixColumns:** Each column is multiplied (modulo $x^4 + 1$) with the fixed polynomial $a(x) = \{03\}x^3 + \{01\}x^2 + \{01\}x + \{02\}$ over GF($2^8$). This further enhances diffusion.

4. **AddRoundKey:** The State is XORed with the round key derived from the key schedule. This mixes key material into the data.

**Block Diagram (text version):**
```
Plaintext → [AddRoundKey K0] → Round 1 [SubBytes → ShiftRows → MixColumns → AddRoundKey K1]
→ Round 2 [...] → ... → Round 10 [SubBytes → ShiftRows → AddRoundKey K10] → Ciphertext
```

---

**Q (b)** Write a Java program to implement AES encryption and decryption using a user-supplied key. Show the expected output. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Java Program):**

```java
import javax.crypto.Cipher;
import javax.crypto.spec.SecretKeySpec;
import java.util.Base64;
import java.util.Scanner;

public class AESLab {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter 16-byte key  : ");
        byte[] keyData = sc.nextLine().getBytes("UTF-8");
        SecretKeySpec key = new SecretKeySpec(keyData, "AES");

        System.out.print("Enter plaintext    : ");
        String plain = sc.nextLine();
        byte[] plainBytes = plain.getBytes("UTF-8");

        Cipher c = Cipher.getInstance("AES/ECB/PKCS5Padding");
        c.init(Cipher.ENCRYPT_MODE, key);
        byte[] enc = c.doFinal(plainBytes);
        String cipherText = Base64.getEncoder().encodeToString(enc);

        c.init(Cipher.DECRYPT_MODE, key);
        byte[] dec = c.doFinal(enc);
        String recovered = new String(dec, "UTF-8");

        System.out.println("Ciphertext         : " + cipherText);
        System.out.println("Decrypted          : " + recovered);
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Sample Output:**
```
Enter 16-byte key  : KTU2024CRYPTO123
Enter plaintext    : Hello AES World
Ciphertext         : ZGVmYXVsdE5lZWRlZFNlY3JldEtleQ==
Decrypted          : Hello AES World
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q (a)** Compare the AES ECB and CBC modes of operation. Why is CBC preferred for most applications? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**ECB (Electronic Codebook):**
- Each plaintext block is encrypted **independently** with the same key.
- **Identical plaintext blocks** produce **identical ciphertext blocks** (deterministic).
- Vulnerable to pattern analysis — an attacker can detect repetition.
- Parallelizable for both encryption and decryption.
- No Initialization Vector (IV) is required.

**CBC (Cipher Block Chaining):**
- Each plaintext block is **XORed with the previous ciphertext block** before encryption.
- The first block is XORed with a random 16-byte **Initialization Vector (IV)**.
- Identical plaintext blocks now produce **different ciphertext blocks** (because the IV/previous block changes).
- Encryption is **sequential**; decryption is parallelizable.
- Provides **semantic security** under CPA (Chosen Plaintext Attack).

**Why CBC is preferred:**
1. **Hides patterns** — Repeated plaintexts yield different ciphertexts.
2. **Avalanche effect** propagates through the chain.
3. **IV randomness** ensures non-deterministic encryption.

**Diagram:**
```
ECB:  P1 → [E] → C1     P2 → [E] → C2
CBC:  P1 ⊕ IV → [E] → C1     P2 ⊕ C1 → [E] → C2
```

**Q (b)** Write a complete Java program demonstrating AES-CBC mode with PKCS5 padding. Include IV generation using `SecureRandom`. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
import javax.crypto.Cipher;
import javax.crypto.KeyGenerator;
import javax.crypto.SecretKey;
import javax.crypto.spec.IvParameterSpec;
import java.util.Base64;
import java.security.SecureRandom;

public class AESCBC {
    public static byte[] generateIV() {
        byte[] iv = new byte[16];
        new SecureRandom().nextBytes(iv);
        return iv;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        // Generate AES key
        KeyGenerator kg = KeyGenerator.getInstance("AES");
        kg.init(128);
        SecretKey key = kg.generateKey();

        // Generate random IV
        byte[] iv = generateIV();
        IvParameterSpec ivSpec = new IvParameterSpec(iv);

        // Plaintext
        String plain = "Confidential KTU Lab Data";
        byte[] p = plain.getBytes("UTF-8");

        // Encrypt
        Cipher c = Cipher.getInstance("AES/CBC/PKCS5Padding");
        c.init(Cipher.ENCRYPT_MODE, key, ivSpec);
        byte[] ct = c.doFinal(p);
        String b64 = Base64.getEncoder().encodeToString(ct);

        // Decrypt
        c.init(Cipher.DECRYPT_MODE, key, ivSpec);
        byte[] pt = c.doFinal(ct);
        String recovered = new String(pt, "UTF-8");

        System.out.println("IV (Base64) : " + Base64.getEncoder().encodeToString(iv));
        System.out.println("Ciphertext  : " + b64);
        System.out.println("Decrypted   : " + recovered);
    }
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting exception handling** — Java cryptography methods throw `Exception`; not using `throws Exception` or `try-catch` will cause **compilation errors** (−2 marks).
> 2. **Confusing ECB and CBC** — Many students write `"AES/ECB/PKCS5Padding"` when the question demands CBC; the IV is **not used in ECB**.
> 3. **Wrong key length** — A user-supplied string shorter or longer than 16 bytes for AES-128 will throw `InvalidKeyException`.
> 4. **Reusing the same Cipher without re-init** — After encryption, you **must** call `cipher.init(DECRYPT_MODE, ...)` before decrypting.
> 5. **Not displaying the decrypted output** — Examiners specifically want proof of round-trip equality.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ AES = **Rijndael**, standardized in **FIPS 197 (2001)**, block size **128 bits**, key sizes **128/192/256**.
- ✅ Number of rounds: **10 / 12 / 14** based on key size.
- ✅ Four round operations: **SubBytes, ShiftRows, MixColumns, AddRoundKey**.
- ✅ Final round **omits MixColumns**; initial round is only **AddRoundKey**.
- ✅ **SubBytes** = S-Box lookup (non-linear, GF($2^8$) inverse + affine transform).
- ✅ **ShiftRows** = cyclic left shift of rows 1, 2, 3 by 1, 2, 3 bytes.
- ✅ **MixColumns** = GF($2^8$) matrix multiplication by polynomial $\{03\}x^3 + \{01\}x^2 + \{01\}x + \{02\}$.
- ✅ **AddRoundKey** = XOR of State with 128-bit round key.
- ✅ Java implementation uses `javax.crypto.Cipher` with `"AES/ECB/PKCS5Padding"` or `"AES/CBC/PKCS5Padding"`.
- ✅ For CBC, a **16-byte IV** is required; use `IvParameterSpec`.
- ✅ ECB is **insecure** for patterned data; CBC, CTR, or GCM are preferred.
- ✅ Base64 is used to **display binary ciphertext** as readable ASCII.
- ✅ `KeyGenerator.getInstance("AES").init(128)` creates a random 128-bit key.
- ✅ `SecretKeySpec` is used when the key is supplied manually.
- ✅ Decryption requires the **same key and IV** as encryption.
- ✅ `cipher.doFinal(byte[])` is the main entry point for both encryption and decryption.
- ✅ AES-256 is approved for **Top Secret** data; AES-128 is sufficient for commercial use.
- ✅ The Rcon array in key expansion: $\text{Rcon}[i] = (\{02\}^{i-1}, \{00\}, \{00\}, \{00\})$ in GF($2^8$).
- ✅ `SecureRandom` is cryptographically strong; never use `java.util.Random` for IVs.
